statement:
  canonical: 'Let k ≥ 2 be a positive integer. For any δ > 0, there exists N such
    that every subset A ⊆ [k]^N with |A| ≥ δ · k^N contains a combinatorial polynomial
    subspace of dimension 1. More precisely, A contains a "polynomial combinatorial
    line," generalizing the notion of combinatorial lines in the density Hales-Jewett
    theorem to polynomial patterns.

    '
  informal: 'The density Hales–Jewett theorem (proved by the Polymath project) says
    that dense subsets of high-dimensional grids contain combinatorial lines. The
    polynomial version asks whether the same holds for polynomial patterns, which
    would vastly generalize Bergelson–Leibman type results.

    '

title: Dixmier Problem
statement_canonical: Is every countable discrete group G whose bounded representations on Hilbert space are all unitarizable (i.e., similar to unitary representations) necessarily amenable? Equivalently, if every uniformly bounded representation pi: G -> B(H) is similar to a unitary representation, must G be amenable? The converse (amenable implies unitarizable) was established by Day and Dixmier.
statement_informal: A group is "unitarizable" if every way of representing it as bounded operators on a Hilbert space can be conjugated to act by unitary (distance- preserving) operators. Amenable groups are always unitarizable. The Dixmier problem asks the converse: is amenability the only reason a group could be unitarizable?

title: Dowling-Wilson Top-Heavy Conjecture
statement_canonical: For any matroid M of rank r, let W_k denote the number of flats of rank k. Then the sequence W_0, W_1, ..., W_r is top-heavy: W_k <= W_{r-k} for all k <= r/2. More generally, the Whitney numbers of the second kind of any matroid satisfy this top-heavy property.
statement_informal: In any matroid, there are at least as many high-rank flats as low-rank flats. The number of subspaces "increases toward the top" of the lattice.

title: Elliott Classification Conjecture
statement_canonical: Simple separable nuclear C*-algebras satisfying the Universal Coefficient Theorem (UCT) are classified by their Elliott invariant, which consists of ordered K-theory (K_0, K_0^+, [1]_{K_0}), K_1-group, and the tracial simplex together with the natural pairing between traces and K_0. In its most general form, the conjecture asks whether all simple separable nuclear C*-algebras satisfy the UCT, and whether the Elliott invariant suffices for classification without the UCT assumption.
statement_informal: C*-algebras are algebras of operators on Hilbert spaces, fundamental in quantum mechanics and noncommutative geometry. Elliott conjectured that the "well-behaved" (nuclear) ones that are also simple (have no ideals) can be completely characterized by a small collection of algebraic and topological invariants. This was proved for those satisfying a technical condition (UCT), but whether all relevant C*-algebras satisfy UCT remains open.

title: Elliott-Halberstam Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every theta < 1 and every A > 0, the sum over q <= x^theta of the maximum over (a,q)=1 of |pi(x; q, a) - li(x)/phi(q)| is O(x / (log x)^A), where pi(x; q, a) counts primes up to x congruent to a mod q. The Bombieri-Vinogradov theorem establishes this for theta < 1/2; the conjecture extends it to all theta < 1.
statement_informal: Primes are equidistributed among arithmetic progressions to a much greater extent than currently provable. The conjecture extends the Bombieri-Vinogradov theorem from moduli up to sqrt(x) to moduli nearly as large as x.

title: Entropy Power Inequality for Dependent Variables
statement_canonical: Does the entropy power inequality extend to Rényi entropies of all orders p >= 1? Specifically, for independent continuous random variables X and Y with finite Rényi entropy of order p, does e^{2 H_p(X+Y)/n} >= e^{2 H_p(X)/n} + e^{2 H_p(Y)/n} hold, where H_p denotes the Rényi entropy of order p and n is the dimension?
statement_informal: The classical entropy power inequality (Shannon, Stam) says that the "entropy power" of the sum of independent random variables is at least the sum of their entropy powers. The open problem asks whether this extends from Shannon entropy to the full family of Rényi entropies.

title: Erdos Conjecture on Arithmetic Progressions
statement_canonical: If A is a set of positive integers such that the sum of reciprocals sum_{a in A} 1/a diverges, then A contains arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions.
statement_informal: If you have a set of positive integers that is "large" in the sense that the sum of their reciprocals is infinite (like the primes), then the set must contain arithmetic progressions of every possible length. This would vastly generalize the Green-Tao theorem on primes.

title: Erdos-Faber-Lovasz Conjecture
statement_canonical: If n complete graphs, each having exactly n vertices, are combined in such a way that any two of them share at most one vertex, then the union graph can be properly colored with n colors. Equivalently, the chromatic index of any linear hypergraph on n vertices is at most n.
statement_informal: Take n complete graphs, each with n vertices. Combine them so that any two share at most one vertex. Then you can color the edges of each complete graph (equivalently, the vertices of the union) using just n colors, so that no two adjacent edges share a color.

title: Erdos-Ginzburg-Ziv Constant for General Groups
statement_canonical: Determine the Davenport constant D(G) for general finite abelian groups G. For a finite abelian group G = Z_{n_1} x Z_{n_2} x ... x Z_{n_r} with n_1 | n_2 | ... | n_r, it is conjectured that D(G) = 1 + sum_{i=1}^{r} (n_i - 1). The Davenport constant D(G) is the smallest integer d such that every sequence of d elements from G contains a nonempty subsequence summing to the identity.
statement_informal: Given a finite abelian group, how long does a sequence of elements need to be before it must contain some nonempty subset that sums to zero? The conjectured answer is determined by the group's invariant factor decomposition, but this remains unproven in general.

statement_informal: In any graph where every vertex has at least 3 neighbors, you can always find a cycle whose length is a power of 2 (i.e., length 2, 4, 8, 16, ...).

title: Erdos-Hajnal Conjecture

statement_informal: If a graph avoids some fixed pattern as an induced subgraph, then it must contain either a very large clique or a very large independent set -- specifically, of polynomial size in the number of vertices, rather than just logarithmic size.

title: Erdos-Ko-Rado for Permutations
statement_canonical: Determine the maximum size of a t-intersecting family of permutations of {1, 2, ..., n}, where a family F of permutations is t-intersecting if every pair sigma, tau in F agrees on at least t points (i.e., |{i : sigma(i) = tau(i)}| >= t). For t = 1, the Deza-Frankl conjecture (proved by Ellis, Friedgut, and Pilpel for large n) states the maximum is (n-1)!. For general t, the conjectured maximum is (n-t)! and the extremal families are cosets of the stabilizer of t points.
statement_informal: Among all collections of permutations where every two permutations agree in at least t positions, how large can the collection be? The conjecture says it is (n-t)!, achieved by fixing t specific positions.

title: Erdos-Mollin-Walsh Conjecture
statement_canonical: There do not exist three consecutive powerful numbers (a powerful number is a positive integer n such that if a prime p divides n, then p^2 also divides n). Equivalently, there is no positive integer n such that n, n+1, and n+2 are all powerful.
statement_informal: A powerful number is one where every prime factor appears at least to the second power (like 4, 8, 9, 25, 27, ...). The conjecture says you can never find three powerful numbers in a row.

title: Erdos-Sos Conjecture

statement_informal: If a graph has enough edges (average degree bigger than k - 2), then it must contain every possible tree with k edges. Having many edges forces all tree shapes to appear.

title: Fatou Conjecture (Density of Hyperbolicity)
statement_canonical: In the space of rational maps of degree d >= 2 on the Riemann sphere, the hyperbolic maps form a dense open subset. Equivalently, for the quadratic family f_c(z) = z^2 + c, the set of parameters c in C for which f_c is hyperbolic (every critical orbit converges to an attracting cycle) is dense in the Mandelbrot set's complement, and hyperbolic components are dense in the Mandelbrot set.
statement_informal: Among all polynomial or rational maps that define dynamical systems on the complex plane, the "well-behaved" (hyperbolic) ones should be the typical case. The conjecture says that for almost any parameter value, the corresponding dynamical system is structurally stable -- chaotic maps that are not hyperbolic should be rare in a topological sense.

title: Frankl's Conjecture: Optimal Abundance Bound
statement_canonical: For a union-closed family F of sets (closed under pairwise union) with F not equal to {empty set}, determine the best constant c such that there always exists an element contained in at least c * |F| sets of F. The union-closed conjecture of Frankl asserts c = 1/2. Gilmer (2022) proved c >= 0.01, and subsequent work improved this to c >= (3 - sqrt(5))/2 ~ 0.382. Closing the gap to 1/2 remains open.
statement_informal: In a family of sets closed under union, there should be some element appearing in at least half the sets. The best proven constant is about 38.2% instead of 50%. What is the true threshold?

    of Frankl asserts c = 1/2. Gilmer (2022) proved c >= 0.01, and subsequent work
    improved this to c >= (3 - sqrt(5))/2 ~ 0.382. Closing the gap to 1/2 remains
    open.

    '
  informal: 'In a family of sets closed under union, there should be some element
    appearing in at least half the sets. The best proven constant is about 38.2% instead
    of 50%. What is the true threshold?

    '

title: Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa Conjecture
statement_canonical: If A is a finite subset of Z^n (or more generally an abelian group) with |A + A| <= K|A| (small doubling), then A can be covered by at most poly(K) translates of a subgroup H with |H| <= |A|. More precisely, there exists a generalized arithmetic progression of dimension at most C(K) containing A, where C(K) is polynomial in K.
statement_informal: Sets with small additive doubling (the sumset is not much larger than the set itself) must have a strong additive structure: they can be efficiently covered by a structured set (generalized arithmetic progression) of comparable size.

title: Furstenberg's x2 x3 Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let mu be a Borel probability measure on the circle R/Z that is invariant under both T_2: x -> 2x (mod 1) and T_3: x -> 3x (mod 1), and is ergodic with respect to the semigroup generated by T_2 and T_3. Then mu is either the Lebesgue measure or is supported on a finite set of rationals. More generally, Furstenberg conjectured that the only nonatomic measure jointly invariant and ergodic under multiplication by 2 and by 3 on R/Z is the Lebesgue measure.
statement_informal: Consider the circle (numbers from 0 to 1 with the ends glued together). The operations "multiply by 2" and "multiply by 3" (mod 1) each preserve certain natural probability distributions. The conjecture says the only "nice" probability distribution that is preserved by both operations simultaneously is the uniform distribution.

title: Freyd's Generating Hypothesis
statement_canonical: If f: X -> Y is a map between finite spectra (finite CW-complexes stably) such that the induced map pi_*(f): pi_*(X) -> pi_*(Y) on stable homotopy groups is the zero map, then f is stably null-homotopic (i.e., f = 0 in the stable homotopy category). Equivalently, the functor pi_* from the stable homotopy category of finite spectra to graded abelian groups is faithful.
statement_informal: In stable homotopy theory, we study spaces "up to suspension." The generating hypothesis says that stable homotopy groups detect everything: if a map between finite spaces induces the zero map on all stable homotopy groups, then the map itself must be zero (stably).

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let p_1 = 2, p_2 = 3, p_3 = 5, ... be the sequence of prime numbers. Define the sequence of iterated absolute differences: d_1(n) = |p_{n+1} - p_n| and d_k(n) = |d_{k-1}(n+1) - d_{k-1}(n)| for k >= 2. Then d_k(1) = 1 for all k >= 1.
statement_informal: Write down the prime numbers. Take the absolute differences of consecutive terms. Repeat this process. The conjecture says the first element of each resulting row of differences is always 1.

title: Graceful Tree Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every tree with n edges admits a graceful labeling. That is, for every tree T with n edges, there exists an injective function f: V(T) -> {0, 1, ..., n} such that the set of values {|f(u) - f(v)| : uv in E(T)} equals {1, 2, ..., n}.
statement_informal: Label the vertices of any tree using distinct numbers from 0 to n (where n is the number of edges). The conjecture claims you can always choose the labels so that the differences along the edges are exactly the numbers 1, 2, ..., n, each appearing exactly once.

title: Grimm's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every positive integer n, if n+1, n+2, ..., n+k are all composite numbers, then there exist distinct primes p_1, p_2, ..., p_k such that p_i divides n+i for each i = 1, 2, ..., k.
statement_informal: Given any run of consecutive composite (non-prime) numbers, one can assign to each composite number in the run a distinct prime factor. No two composites in the run need share the same assigned prime.

title: Grothendieck's Standard Conjectures
statement_canonical: Grothendieck's standard conjectures consist of four related conjectures for smooth projective varieties: (A) the Lefschetz standard conjecture - the inverse of the hard Lefschetz isomorphism in cohomology is induced by an algebraic correspondence; (B) the conjecture of Hodge type - a certain quadratic form on algebraic cycles defined via intersection pairing is positive definite on primitive classes; (C) the Kunneth standard conjecture - the Kunneth components of the diagonal are algebraic; (D) numerical and homological equivalence coincide for algebraic cycles.
statement_informal: These conjectures assert that deep topological structures on the cohomology of algebraic varieties (like the Lefschetz decomposition) have purely algebraic origins, bridging topology and algebra in a canonical way.

title: Gyarfas-Sumner Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every tree T, there exists a function f_T such that every graph G with no induced copy of T satisfies chi(G) <= f_T(omega(G)), where chi(G) is the chromatic number and omega(G) is the clique number. In other words, every tree is chi-binding: forbidding it as an induced subgraph bounds the chromatic number in terms of the clique number.
statement_informal: If you forbid a specific tree as an induced subgraph, then graphs cannot have a huge chromatic number unless they also have a large clique. Trees are "well-behaved" as forbidden induced subgraphs in this sense.

  chi-bounded (e.g., cycles of odd length), making the role of trees special.

  '

title: Hadamard Conjecture
statement_canonical: A Hadamard matrix of order n is an n x n matrix H with entries in {-1, +1} satisfying H H^T = n I_n. Hadamard's conjecture asserts that a Hadamard matrix of order n exists for every positive integer n that is divisible by 4 (and also for n = 1 and n = 2).
statement_informal: A Hadamard matrix is a square matrix whose entries are all +1 or -1, and whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Such matrices can only exist in sizes 1, 2, or multiples of 4. The conjecture is that they exist for every multiple of 4.

title: Hadwiger Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every integer t >= 1, every graph with no K_t minor is (t-1)-colorable. Equivalently, if a graph G requires at least t colors for a proper vertex coloring (i.e., chi(G) >= t), then G contains K_t as a minor.
statement_informal: If a graph needs at least t colors to color its vertices (so that no two adjacent vertices share a color), then it must contain a "squished" version of the complete graph on t vertices as a substructure.

    no K_t minor is O(t (log t)^beta)-colorable for some beta < 1.'
  year: 2019

statement:
  canonical: 'For every integer t >= 1, every graph with no K_t minor is (t-1)-colorable.
    Equivalently, if a graph G requires at least t colors for a proper vertex coloring
    (i.e., chi(G) >= t), then G contains K_t as a minor.

    '
  informal: 'If a graph needs at least t colors to color its vertices (so that no
    two adjacent vertices share a color), then it must contain a "squished" version
    of the complete graph on t vertices as a substructure.

    '

title: Hadwiger-Nelson Problem
statement_canonical: What is the minimum number of colors needed to color all points of the Euclidean plane such that no two points at distance exactly 1 from each other share the same color? Equivalently, what is the chromatic number chi(G) of the unit-distance graph of the plane? It is known that 5 <= chi <= 7.
statement_informal: How many colors do you need to paint the plane so that no two points exactly 1 unit apart share the same color? The answer is known to be between 5 and 7, but the exact value remains unknown.

  the lower bound from 4 to 5, the first improvement since the problem was posed.

  '

title: Hall's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every epsilon > 0, there exists a constant c(epsilon) > 0 such that if x and y are positive integers with x^3 != y^2, then |x^3 - y^2| > c(epsilon) * x^{1/2 - epsilon}. The strong form asserts that |x^3 - y^2| > c * x^{1/2} for an absolute constant c > 0.
statement_informal: When a perfect cube and a perfect square are not equal, the gap between them grows at least roughly as the square root of the cube. It is hard for a cube and a square to be very close together without being equal.

title: Halpern-Lauchli Theorem Extensions to Uncountable Cardinals
statement_canonical: The Halpern-Lauchli theorem states that for any finite number d of perfect binary trees T_1, ..., T_d of height omega, and any finite coloring of the level product Union_n (T_1(n) x ... x T_d(n)), there exist strong subtrees S_i subset T_i of height omega such that the level product of S_1, ..., S_d is monochromatic. The open problem asks: does the analogous statement hold when omega is replaced by an uncountable cardinal kappa? In particular, does it hold at kappa = omega_1?
statement_informal: The Halpern-Lauchli theorem is a powerful Ramsey-type result about coloring products of trees. It works beautifully for trees of countable height. The open question is whether this deep combinatorial principle extends to trees of uncountable height, which requires fundamentally new techniques or potentially new axioms.

title: Happel's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let A be a finite-dimensional algebra over an algebraically closed field k. If the derived category D^b(mod-A) of bounded complexes of finitely generated A-modules has no loops in its Auslander-Reiten quiver and has a tilting object, then A is derived equivalent to a hereditary algebra.
statement_informal: If the derived category of a finite-dimensional algebra is well-behaved (has no self-extensions of a certain type and possesses a tilting object), then the algebra should be essentially as simple as a hereditary algebra (one with no higher extension groups).

title: Hardy-Littlewood k-Tuple Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let H = {h_1, h_2, ..., h_k} be an admissible k-tuple of non-negative integers (meaning for every prime p, the set H does not cover all residue classes modulo p). Let pi_H(x) denote the number of integers n <= x such that n + h_1, n + h_2, ..., n + h_k are all prime. Then pi_H(x) ~ S(H) * x / (log x)^k as x -> infinity, where S(H) = prod_{p prime} (1 - nu_H(p)/p) / (1 - 1/p)^k and nu_H(p) is the number of distinct residues of H modulo p. In particular, pi_H(x) -> infinity.
statement_informal: Consider any pattern of gaps between primes that is not ruled out by simple divisibility obstructions. The conjecture predicts that this pattern appears infinitely often among the primes, and gives an exact formula for how frequently it appears.

title: Hilbert's 16th Problem (Second Part)
statement_canonical: For a polynomial vector field of degree n in the plane, dx/dt = P_n(x,y), dy/dt = Q_n(x,y), where P_n and Q_n are polynomials of degree at most n, determine a uniform upper bound H(n) on the number of limit cycles (isolated periodic orbits), and investigate their possible configurations. In particular, is H(n) finite for each n?
statement_informal: Consider a system of two differential equations in the plane where the right-hand sides are polynomials of degree n. The question asks: what is the maximum number of closed loops (limit cycles) that the trajectories of such a system can form? Even for quadratic systems (n=2), this remains unsolved.

title: Hopf Conjecture (Positive Sectional Curvature)
statement_canonical: Let M be a closed (compact, without boundary) Riemannian manifold of even dimension 2n with positive sectional curvature. Then the Euler characteristic chi(M) > 0. Equivalently, does positive sectional curvature in even dimensions force the Euler characteristic to be positive?
statement_informal: In Riemannian geometry, sectional curvature measures how curved a space is in each 2-dimensional direction. If a compact even-dimensional manifold curves positively in every direction, Hopf conjectured that a topological invariant called the Euler characteristic must be positive. This is known in dimensions 2 and 4 but open in general.

title: Hot Spots Conjecture
statement_canonical: For a bounded, connected planar domain D with smooth boundary, the second Neumann eigenfunction of the Laplacian attains its maximum and minimum on the boundary of D. Equivalently, if heat diffuses in D with insulating boundary conditions, the hottest and coldest points eventually migrate to the boundary for generic initial conditions.
statement_informal: If you heat a flat, insulated metal plate, the hottest and coldest spots will eventually end up on the boundary, not in the interior. This is conjectured to hold for all convex domains, but the general case for simply connected domains is open.

title: Non-abelian Iwasawa Main Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let F_infty / F be a p-adic Lie extension of a number field F with Galois group G = Gal(F_infty / F), a compact p-adic Lie group (not necessarily abelian). The non-abelian Iwasawa main conjecture asserts that the characteristic element of the dual Selmer group (as a module over the Iwasawa algebra Lambda(G)) equals, up to a unit, a p-adic L-function element in an appropriate K_1 group of the Iwasawa algebra. This generalizes the classical abelian Iwasawa main conjecture (proved by Mazur-Wiles and Wiles) to non-commutative Galois extensions.
statement_informal: Iwasawa theory connects algebraic objects (ideal class groups in towers of number fields) with analytic objects (p-adic L-functions). The classical theory works for commutative (abelian) towers. The non-abelian conjecture extends this to non-commutative towers, requiring fundamentally new algebraic machinery involving noncommutative rings.

title: Jacobian Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let F = (F_1, ..., F_n): C^n -> C^n be a polynomial map such that the Jacobian determinant det(dF_i/dx_j) is a nonzero constant. Then F is a polynomial automorphism, i.e., F has a polynomial inverse. Equivalently, if F: C^n -> C^n is a polynomial map with constant nonzero Jacobian determinant, then F is bijective.
statement_informal: If you have a system of polynomial equations in n variables whose Jacobian determinant (a measure of local invertibility) is everywhere nonzero, then the system must have a global polynomial inverse -- you can solve for the original variables using polynomials.

title: Jakobson's Conjecture (Prevalence of Stochastic Behavior)
statement_canonical: For a generic smooth one-parameter family of unimodal maps f_a: [0,1] -> [0,1] (such as the quadratic family f_a(x) = ax(1-x)), the set of parameters a for which f_a admits an absolutely continuous invariant probability measure (a.c.i.p.) -- i.e., exhibits stochastic behavior -- has positive Lebesgue measure. More broadly (Palis conjecture), for typical one-dimensional dynamical systems, the parameter space decomposes into a set where the system is hyperbolic (structurally stable) and a set where it supports an a.c.i.p., and together these sets have full Lebesgue measure.
statement_informal: Consider the family of maps x -> ax(1-x) as the parameter a varies. For some values of a the dynamics are orderly (periodic), and for others they are chaotic with a natural probability distribution. The conjecture predicts that "almost all" parameter values fall into one of these two categories -- there is essentially no third type of behavior.

title: Kadison's Similarity Problem
statement_canonical: Let A be a C*-algebra and let pi: A -> B(H) be a bounded unital homomorphism into the bounded operators on a Hilbert space H. Is pi necessarily similar to a *-homomorphism? That is, does there exist an invertible operator S in B(H) such that rho(a) = S^{-1} pi(a) S is a *-homomorphism for all a in A?
statement_informal: When you represent a C*-algebra (a special algebra of operators) as operators on a Hilbert space, a well-behaved representation preserves the adjoint operation. Kadison asked whether every bounded (but not necessarily adjoint-preserving) representation can be "corrected" by a change of basis to become adjoint-preserving.

title: Kadison-Singer Problem Extensions (Infinite-Dimensional Paving)
statement_canonical: Does the paving property hold for operators on infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces relative to more general maximal abelian subalgebras (MASAs) beyond l^infty? Specifically, for a type II_1 factor M with a diffuse MASA A, does every operator T in M with E_A(T) = 0 (where E_A is the conditional expectation onto A) satisfy an appropriate paving condition? More broadly, can the Marcus-Spielman-Srivastava framework be extended to infinite-dimensional settings?
statement_informal: The original Kadison-Singer problem (solved in 2013) asked about extending certain linear functionals on diagonal operators. The solved version dealt with finite-dimensional approximations. The remaining open extensions ask whether similar "paving" (breaking matrices into small blocks that are nearly diagonal) works in truly infinite-dimensional or continuous (type II_1) settings.

title: Kalai's Conjecture on Noise Sensitivity of Boolean Functions
statement_canonical: Consider a monotone Boolean function f: {0,1}^n -> {0,1} that is balanced (Pr[f=1] ~ 1/2). If f has no "influential" variable (the influence of each variable is at most epsilon), then f is noise-sensitive: for any delta > 0, the correlation between f(x) and f(x^delta) tends to 0 as epsilon -> 0, where x^delta is obtained from x by independently flipping each bit with probability delta. More precisely, f is noise-sensitive if and only if the sum of squared Fourier coefficients above level k tends to zero for fixed k.
statement_informal: Boolean functions where no single variable has much influence are easily corrupted by random noise — a small amount of random bit-flipping destroys the correlation with the original output.

title: Keating-Snaith Conjecture
statement_canonical: For each positive real k, the 2k-th moment of the Riemann zeta function on the critical line satisfies (1/T) integral_0^T |zeta(1/2 + it)|^{2k} dt ~ c_k (log T)^{k^2} as T -> infinity, where c_k is an explicit constant predicted by random matrix theory involving Barnes' G-function: c_k = g_k * a_k, with g_k from the CUE ensemble and a_k an arithmetic factor involving primes.
statement_informal: The moments of the Riemann zeta function along the critical line grow like specific powers of log T, with precise constants predicted by modeling the zeta function as a random unitary matrix.

title: Kervaire Invariant Problem
statement_canonical: Does there exist a framed manifold of dimension 126 with Kervaire invariant one? The Kervaire invariant θ_j ∈ π^s_{2^{j+1}-2} exists only in dimensions 2^{j+1} - 2. Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel (2016) proved that framed manifolds with Kervaire invariant one do not exist in dimensions 2^{j+1} - 2 for j ≥ 7 (i.e., dimensions ≥ 254). The problem is resolved positively in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, and 62, leaving dimension 126 as the sole remaining open case.
statement_informal: A classical topological invariant called the Kervaire invariant is known to be nontrivial in certain specific dimensions. The question of whether it is nontrivial in dimension 126 is the last unsettled case.

statement:
  canonical: 'Does there exist a framed manifold of dimension 126 with Kervaire invariant
    one? The Kervaire invariant θ_j ∈ π^s_{2^{j+1}-2} exists only in dimensions 2^{j+1}
    - 2. Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel (2016) proved that framed manifolds with Kervaire
    invariant one do not exist in dimensions 2^{j+1} - 2 for j ≥ 7 (i.e., dimensions
    ≥ 254). The problem is resolved positively in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, and 62,
    leaving dimension 126 as the sole remaining open case.

    '
  informal: 'A classical topological invariant called the Kervaire invariant is known
    to be nontrivial in certain specific dimensions. The question of whether it is
    nontrivial in dimension 126 is the last unsettled case.

    '

title: Khintchine's Flatness Conjecture
statement_canonical: Determine the optimal flatness constant f(n): the smallest constant such that every convex body K in R^n that contains no integer lattice point in its interior has width at most f(n) in some lattice direction. The flatness theorem (Khintchine, 1948) guarantees f(n) is finite. The conjecture is that f(n) = O(n^{2/3}). Current best bounds give f(n) = O(n^{3/2}) (Banaszczyk) and f(n) = Omega(n^{1/3}) (Kannan-Lovász).
statement_informal: How "thin" must a convex body be in some direction if it avoids all integer points? The precise growth rate of the flatness constant with dimension is unknown.

title: KLS Conjecture
statement_canonical: For any log-concave probability distribution mu on R^n, the Cheeger isoperimetric constant psi_mu satisfies psi_mu >= c * sigma_mu, where sigma_mu is the square root of the smallest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix and c > 0 is a universal constant independent of n.
statement_informal: For any log-concave probability distribution in high dimensions, the isoperimetric constant (measuring the minimal boundary-to-volume ratio of partitions) is controlled by the variance. The conjecture says no dimension-dependent loss occurs.

title: Kontsevich-Zagier Conjecture
statement_canonical: If two periods (numbers expressible as integrals of algebraic functions over semi-algebraic domains defined over Q) are equal, then there exists a finite sequence of elementary operations — additivity of integrals, change of variables with algebraic Jacobians, and Stokes' theorem — that transforms one integral representation into the other.
statement_informal: All relations between periods (a class of numbers including pi, log(2), and values of the zeta function at integers) should follow from three basic rules of calculus: linearity, substitution, and Stokes' theorem.

title: Kothe Conjecture
statement_canonical: If a ring R has no nonzero two-sided nil ideal, then R has no nonzero one-sided nil ideal. Equivalently, the sum of two left nil ideals of any ring is again a nil ideal. This can also be stated as: the upper nil radical of any ring contains every left nil ideal.
statement_informal: In a ring (an algebraic structure with addition and multiplication), a nil ideal is one where every element, when raised to some power, gives zero. The conjecture asks: if there is no "global" nil ideal, can there still be a "one-sided" nil ideal?

title: Lander-Parkin-Selfridge Conjecture
statement_canonical: If the sum a_1^k + a_2^k + ... + a_s^k = b_1^k + b_2^k + ... + b_t^k holds with all a_i, b_j positive integers and k >= 2, then s + t >= k. Equivalently, the Diophantine equation x_1^k + x_2^k + ... + x_m^k = 0 in nonzero integers requires m >= k + 1 terms.
statement_informal: When expressing an equation of equal sums of k-th powers on both sides, the total number of terms on both sides combined must be at least k. You cannot write an identity with too few k-th power terms.

title: Lang's Conjecture on Rational Points
statement_canonical: Let X be a smooth projective variety of general type defined over a number field K. Then the set of K-rational points X(K) is not Zariski dense in X. More precisely, there exists a proper closed subvariety Z subset X such that X(K) subset Z(K), i.e., all but finitely many rational points lie on a proper subvariety.
statement_informal: Varieties of general type are the "most curved" algebraic varieties, the higher-dimensional analogues of hyperbolic curves. Lang conjectured that such varieties over number fields can only have a "sparse" set of rational points -- they must all be confined to a fixed lower-dimensional subset. This vastly generalizes Faltings' theorem (Mordell conjecture) from curves to higher dimensions.

title: Langlands Functoriality Conjecture
statement_canonical: For reductive groups G and H over a number field F, every homomorphism of L-groups r: ^L H -> ^L G should induce a transfer of automorphic representations from H to G. More precisely, if pi is an automorphic representation of H(A_F) with L-parameter phi_pi, then there exists an automorphic representation Pi of G(A_F) with L-parameter r ∘ phi_pi.
statement_informal: There should be a systematic way to transfer automorphic forms between different algebraic groups, governed by homomorphisms between their dual groups. This "functoriality principle" is the central organizing conjecture of the Langlands program.

title: Large Cardinal Consistency Strength Hierarchy
statement_canonical: Construct an inner model (a canonical model of set theory analogous to Godel's L) for a supercompact cardinal. More broadly, develop inner model theory for the large cardinal hierarchy at and beyond the level of supercompact cardinals, establishing the consistency strength of these axioms relative to each other and to combinatorial set-theoretic principles.
statement_informal: Large cardinal axioms form a linear hierarchy of increasingly strong assumptions about the universe of sets. Inner model theory provides canonical models that "tame" these axioms. Building inner models has succeeded up to Woodin cardinals, but extending the theory to supercompact cardinals and beyond remains a major open challenge.

  have been constructed up to the level of Woodin cardinals (by Steel, Mitchell, and
  others), but extending beyond to supercompact and stronger cardinals is considered
  one of the hardest and most important open problems in mathematical logic. Success
  would illuminate the structure of the set- theoretic universe and the relationship
  between different foundational axioms.

  '

title: Lattice Distortion Problem
statement_canonical: For finite lattices L of size n (as partially ordered sets), what is the minimum distortion required to embed the lattice metric (shortest path distance in the Hasse diagram) into L^1 (Manhattan distance) space? Specifically, determine the worst-case distortion c_1(L_n) = inf_f sup_{x,y} d_{L^1}(f(x),f(y)) / d_L(x,y) * sup_{x,y} d_L(x,y) / d_{L^1}(f(x),f(y)) over all lattices of size n and all embeddings f.
statement_informal: How well can the metric structure of lattices be preserved when embedded into familiar geometric spaces? The worst-case distortion for embedding lattice metrics into L^1 is poorly understood.

title: Legendre's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every positive integer n, there exists a prime number p such that n^2 < p < (n+1)^2. Equivalently, there is always at least one prime between consecutive perfect squares.
statement_informal: Between any two consecutive perfect squares (like 100 and 121, or 144 and 169), there is always at least one prime number. Despite strong numerical evidence, this has never been proved.

  canonical: 'For every positive integer n, there exists a prime number p such that

    perfect squares.

  informal: 'Between any two consecutive perfect squares (like 100 and 121, or 144
    and 169), there is always at least one prime number. Despite strong numerical
    evidence, this has never been proved.

    '

title: Lehmer's Conjecture (Mahler Measure)
statement_canonical: There exists an absolute constant c > 0 such that for every monic irreducible polynomial P(x) in Z[x] that is not a cyclotomic polynomial, the Mahler measure M(P) = prod_{i} max(1, |alpha_i|), where the alpha_i are the roots of P, satisfies M(P) >= 1 + c. Equivalently, the infimum of Mahler measures of non-cyclotomic integer polynomials is strictly greater than 1, and is conjectured to be Lehmer's number, M(x^10 + x^9 - x^7 - x^6 - x^5 - x^4 - x^3 + x + 1) ~ 1.17628.
statement_informal: The Mahler measure of a polynomial is a way to measure its "size" using its roots. For cyclotomic polynomials (whose roots are roots of unity), this measure equals 1. Lehmer asked whether there is a gap -- is there a minimum amount by which the Mahler measure of any other integer polynomial must exceed 1?

title: Lehmer's Totient Problem
statement_canonical: There is no composite positive integer n such that Euler's totient function phi(n) divides n - 1. Equivalently, if phi(n) | (n - 1), then n is prime.
statement_informal: Euler's totient function phi(n) counts how many integers from 1 to n are coprime to n. For primes p, phi(p) = p - 1, which divides p - 1. The question asks whether any composite number can also have the property that phi(n) divides n - 1.

title: Leray's Conjecture on Self-Similar Blow-Up
statement_canonical: There are no non-trivial self-similar blow-up solutions to the three-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. Specifically, if u(x,t) = (T-t)^{-1/2} U(x / (T-t)^{1/2}) is a solution to the Navier-Stokes equations that blows up at time T, then U must be identically zero (no non-zero self-similar profile exists with finite energy).
statement_informal: If the Navier-Stokes equations describing fluid flow were to develop a singularity (blow-up), it cannot happen in a self-similar way -- the simplest type of singularity is ruled out.

title: Strongly Polynomial Linear Programming
statement_canonical: Does there exist a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for linear programming? That is, an algorithm whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in the number of variables n and number of constraints m only, independent of the bit-size of the coefficients. Current polynomial-time algorithms (ellipsoid method, interior point methods) are weakly polynomial — their complexity depends on log of the input numbers.
statement_informal: Can linear programs be solved in a number of steps that depends only on the number of variables and constraints, not on the size of the numbers involved?

title: List Coloring Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every multigraph G, the list chromatic index equals the chromatic index: chi'_l(G) = chi'(G). Equivalently, if the edges of a graph can be properly colored with k colors, then they can be properly colored even if each edge has its own list of k available colors.
statement_informal: Edge coloring with lists is no harder than ordinary edge coloring. If k colors suffice for a proper edge coloring, then you can still color properly even when each edge is restricted to its own palette of k colors.

title: Log-Brunn-Minkowski Conjecture
statement_canonical: For origin-symmetric convex bodies K and L in R^n and lambda in [0,1], the inequality Vol((1-lambda) cdot K +_0 lambda cdot L) >= Vol(K)^{1-lambda} Vol(L)^lambda holds, where +_0 denotes the log-Minkowski addition (geometric mean of support functions). Equivalently, the cone-volume measure satisfies a log-concavity property stronger than the classical Brunn-Minkowski inequality.
statement_informal: The conjecture proposes a multiplicative (logarithmic) strengthening of the classical Brunn-Minkowski inequality for origin-symmetric convex bodies, replacing the usual Minkowski addition with a geometric-mean operation on support functions.

  '

title: Lonely Runner Conjecture
statement_canonical: Consider k >= 2 runners on a circular track of circumference 1, starting at the same point and running with pairwise distinct constant speeds. For each runner, there exists a time at which that runner is at distance at least 1/k from all other runners. Equivalently, for every set of k-1 positive integers v_1, ..., v_{k-1}, there exists a real number t such that for all i, the fractional part of t * v_i is in [1/k, (k-1)/k].
statement_informal: Imagine k runners jogging around a circular track, all starting at the same point but at different constant speeds. The conjecture says that each runner will, at some point in time, be "lonely" -- separated from every other runner by at least 1/k of the track's length.

title: Mahler Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every centrally symmetric convex body K in R^n, the volume product Vol(K) * Vol(K^o) >= 4^n / n!, where K^o is the polar body of K. Equality is conjectured to hold if and only if K is a hypercube (or its affine image). In the non-symmetric case, the conjectured minimum is (n+1)^{n+1} / (n!)^2, achieved by the simplex.
statement_informal: Among all centrally symmetric convex bodies, the hypercube (and its dual, the cross-polytope) should minimize the product of the volume of the body and the volume of its polar dual.

title: Matrix Mortality Problem
statement_canonical: Given a finite set of n x n integer matrices {M_1, ..., M_k}, is it decidable whether there exists a product M_{i_1} M_{i_2} ... M_{i_m} equal to the zero matrix? This is known to be undecidable for n >= 3 (Paterson, 1970). The question is open for 2x2 matrices: given a finite set of 2x2 integer matrices, is the mortality problem (does some product equal the zero matrix?) decidable?
statement_informal: Given a collection of 2x2 integer matrices, can we always determine whether some product of these matrices (with repetition allowed) gives the zero matrix?

statement_canonical: The Mandelbrot set M = {c in C : the orbit of 0 under f_c(z) = z^2 + c is bounded} is locally connected. That is, for every point c in M and every open set U containing c, there exists a connected open set V with c in V subset U intersect M.
statement_informal: The Mandelbrot set is the famous fractal that catalogs the behavior of quadratic maps z -> z^2 + c. The MLC conjecture asserts that this set has no "infinitely thin filaments" -- every point can be surrounded by an arbitrarily small connected neighborhood within the set.

  over shapes and continuous motions. The best known lower bound is Gerver''s sofa
  (area ≈ 2.2195) from 1992, and Romik''s ambidextrous sofa handles a symmetric variant.

  the proof is not yet fully verified.

  '

title: Navier-Stokes Existence and Smoothness
statement_canonical: In three spatial dimensions, given any initial velocity field v_0(x) that is smooth and divergence-free (and decays sufficiently rapidly at infinity), prove one of the following: (A) There exist smooth functions v(x,t) (velocity) and p(x,t) (pressure) defined for all t > 0 that satisfy the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations dv/dt + (v . nabla)v = -nabla p + nu Delta v, div v = 0, with v(x, 0) = v_0(x), and v has bounded energy for all t > 0; or (B) There exists a smooth, divergence-free initial condition v_0 for which no such smooth solution exists for all t > 0 (i.e., singularities develop in finite time).
statement_informal: The Navier-Stokes equations describe the motion of fluids like water and air. In three dimensions, we do not know whether smooth solutions always exist for all time, or whether the fluid can develop a "singularity" where the velocity becomes infinite. This problem asks for a definitive answer.

  to the Navier-Stokes regularity problem (one of the Millennium Prize Problems).
  Understanding Euler blow-up would shed light on turbulence, energy cascades, and
  the physical limits of classical fluid mechanics. Recent work by Elgindi (2021)
  established blow-up for certain classes of initial data with limited regularity,
  and Chen-Hou (2023) provided computer-assisted proofs of blow-up for C^{1,alpha}
  data.

  '

title: Neggers-Stanley Conjecture
statement_canonical: For a finite poset P, the order polynomial Omega(P, t) (counting the number of order-preserving maps from P to {1, 2, ..., t}) has only real zeros when expressed in the basis of binomial coefficients. Equivalently, the W-polynomial (a transformation of the order polynomial) has only real roots. The original Neggers conjecture (for naturally labeled posets) was disproved; the modified version due to Stanley remains open.
statement_informal: A polynomial that counts the number of ways to assign values from {1,...,t} to elements of a partially ordered set in an order-respecting way should, after a suitable transformation, have only real roots.

title: Consistency of Quine's New Foundations
statement_canonical: Is Quine's set theory NF (New Foundations) consistent relative to standard set-theoretic axioms? NF is the set theory with a universal set, stratified comprehension, and extensionality. The question is whether NF is consistent relative to ZFC or to some natural extension of ZFC. Holmes (2012+) has claimed a proof of consistency of NF, which is being formalized in Lean, but the proof has not yet been fully verified and published in a peer-reviewed journal.
statement_informal: Quine proposed an alternative set theory in 1937 that allows a universal set (the set of all sets). Despite decades of study, nobody has been able to prove whether this theory is consistent. A claimed proof exists but has not been fully verified.

title: Nonnegative Rank vs Extension Complexity
statement_canonical: For a polytope P, the extension complexity xc(P) is the minimum number of facets of any polytope Q that linearly projects onto P. Yannakakis showed xc(P) equals the nonnegative rank of the slack matrix of P. The open question is to determine the extension complexity of fundamental polytopes: in particular, does the matching polytope of K_n have extension complexity 2^{Omega(n)}, and what is the extension complexity of the TSP polytope?
statement_informal: How efficiently can combinatorial optimization polytopes be represented as projections of simpler polytopes? Understanding this determines the power of linear programming for combinatorial optimization.

title: Novikov Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let M be a closed oriented smooth manifold with fundamental group π. For any class x ∈ H^*(Bπ; Q) in the rational cohomology of the classifying space, the higher signature ⟨L(M) ∪ f^*(x), [M]⟩ is an oriented homotopy invariant, where L(M) is the Hirzebruch L-class and f: M → Bπ classifies the universal cover.
statement_informal: Certain numerical invariants of manifolds defined using their fundamental group (called higher signatures) should be preserved when one manifold is continuously deformed into another in a way that preserves orientation.

title: Overfull Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let G be a graph with maximum degree Delta(G) > n/3, where n = |V(G)|. Then the chromatic index chi'(G) = Delta(G) + 1 (i.e., G is Class 2) if and only if G contains an overfull subgraph H with Delta(H) = Delta(G). A graph H is overfull if |E(H)| > Delta(H) * floor(|V(H)|/2).
statement_informal: For graphs with sufficiently high maximum degree, the only reason a graph needs one extra color for edge-coloring (beyond its maximum degree) is if it contains a dense substructure (an overfull subgraph) that forces this.

  provide a complete characterization of the chromatic index for graphs with large
  maximum degree. By Vizing''s theorem, every simple graph has chromatic index Delta
  or Delta + 1. Determining which case holds is NP-complete in general, but the Overfull
  conjecture implies it can be decided in polynomial time for dense graphs.

  '

title: Pierce-Birkhoff Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every piecewise-polynomial function f: R^n -> R (i.e., a continuous function such that R^n can be partitioned into finitely many closed semialgebraic regions on each of which f agrees with a polynomial) can be expressed as a finite combination of polynomials using only the operations of max and min. Equivalently, f belongs to the lattice generated by the polynomial ring R[x_1,...,x_n] under sup and inf operations.
statement_informal: Any continuous function that is polynomial on each piece of a finite partition of R^n can be written using max and min of finitely many polynomials.

title: Pillai's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every positive integer k, there are only finitely many pairs of perfect powers (a^m, b^n) with a, b, m, n >= 2 and |a^m - b^n| = k. Equivalently, the gaps between consecutive perfect powers tend to infinity.
statement_informal: Perfect powers are numbers like 4, 8, 9, 16, 25, 27, ... (squares, cubes, etc.). Pillai's conjecture says that as you go further out, consecutive perfect powers get increasingly far apart, so any fixed gap k occurs only finitely many times.

title: Polya Conjecture on Dirichlet Characters
statement_canonical: For a non-principal Dirichlet character chi modulo q, the maximum of |sum_{n<=x} chi(n)| over all x should be bounded by O(sqrt(q) * log log q). The classical Polya-Vinogradov inequality gives the bound O(sqrt(q) * log q). The conjecture asserts that the log q factor can be replaced by log log q, at least for primitive characters.
statement_informal: Sums of Dirichlet characters (which generalize the Legendre symbol) should be much smaller than what the classical Polya-Vinogradov inequality proves. The conjecture sharpens the best known general bound.

  R(2,k), R(3,k) for small k, and R(4,4)=18 are known exactly. Finding R(5,5) has
  been called one of the hardest open problems in combinatorics. Paul Erdos famously
  said that if aliens demanded we compute R(5,5) or face destruction, we should mobilize
  all our computers; but if they asked for R(6,6), we should prepare to fight.

  '

title: Anderson Localization in Higher Dimensions
statement_canonical: Consider the Anderson model H = -Δ + V on ℓ^2(Z^d) where V is an i.i.d. random potential with bounded distribution. For d ≥ 3 and sufficiently small disorder (small coupling constant λ), does the operator H have absolutely continuous spectrum and extended (delocalized) eigenstates? Prove the existence of a mobility edge separating localized and extended states in dimensions d ≥ 3.
statement_informal: In a disordered quantum system on a lattice, particles can get trapped (localized) by randomness. In low dimensions or with strong disorder, all states are localized. In higher dimensions with weak disorder, there should be a transition where some states become delocalized — waves can propagate. Proving this transition exists is one of the great open problems in mathematical physics.

title: Reconstruction Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every simple finite undirected graph G on n >= 3 vertices is determined up to isomorphism by its deck, the multiset of (isomorphism classes of) vertex-deleted subgraphs {G - v : v in V(G)}. In other words, if two graphs G and H with at least 3 vertices have the same deck, then G and H are isomorphic.
statement_informal: Imagine you have a graph and you create a collection of smaller graphs, each obtained by removing one vertex (and all its edges). The conjecture says that you can always figure out the original graph from this collection of subgraphs, as long as it has at least 3 vertices.

    p admit a resolution of singularities? That is, given an integral scheme X of
    finite type over a field k with char(k) = p > 0, does there exist a proper birational
    morphism Y → X with Y smooth?

    '
  informal: 'Every algebraic variety (solution set of polynomial equations) over a
    field of characteristic zero can be "desingularized" — replaced by a smooth variety
    that is birationally equivalent. Can the same always be done when the characteristic
    of the field is a prime number?

    '

title: Riemann Hypothesis
statement_canonical: All non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function zeta(s) = sum_{n=1}^{infinity} n^{-s} have real part equal to 1/2. That is, if zeta(s) = 0 and s is not a negative even integer, then Re(s) = 1/2.
statement_informal: The Riemann zeta function encodes deep information about the distribution of prime numbers. The hypothesis states that all its "interesting" zeros lie on a single vertical line in the complex plane (the critical line Re(s) = 1/2).

title: Ringel's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every tree T with n edges, the complete graph K_{2n+1} can be decomposed into 2n+1 edge-disjoint copies of T. Equivalently, every tree with n edges packs 2n+1 times into the complete graph K_{2n+1}.
statement_informal: Any tree with n edges can tile the complete graph on 2n+1 vertices perfectly -- the edges of the complete graph can be partitioned into 2n+1 copies of the tree with no overlaps.

title: Rota-Welsh Conjecture (Extensions)
statement_canonical: The original Rota-Welsh conjecture — that the absolute values of coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of any matroid form a log-concave sequence — was proved by Adiprasito, Huh, and Katz (2018). The extended conjecture asks whether the same sequence is ultra-log-concave: |a_k|^2 >= (1 + 1/k)(1 + 1/(r-k)) |a_{k-1}| |a_{k+1}| for all relevant k, where r is the rank. This stronger property remains open for general matroids.
statement_informal: The coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of a matroid are log-concave (proved). The remaining open question is whether a stronger "ultra-log-concavity" property holds, which would imply even tighter control on coefficient ratios.

title: Rota's Basis Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let B_1, B_2, ..., B_n be n bases of a rank-n matroid M. Then one can always find n disjoint transversals of (B_1, ..., B_n), each of which is itself a basis of M. Equivalently, if the elements of the n bases are arranged as rows of an n x n matrix, one can permute within each row so that every column is also a basis.
statement_informal: Given n bases of a matroid, each with n elements, arrange them in a grid. Can you always rearrange each row so that every column also forms a basis? Rota conjectured yes.

title: Ryser's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every Latin square of odd order n has a transversal (a set of n entries, one from each row and one from each column, with all symbols distinct). More generally, the Ryser-Brualdi-Stein conjecture asserts that every Latin square of order n has a partial transversal of size at least n - 1.
statement_informal: In an n x n Latin square (where each symbol appears exactly once in each row and column), can you always find n entries, one per row and one per column, that are all different? Ryser conjectured yes when n is odd; the Brualdi-Stein extension asks for n - 1 such entries in all cases.

title: Sarnak's Mobius Disjointness Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every continuous function f on a compact metric space X and every homeomorphism T: X -> X with zero topological entropy, and for every point x in X, the sum (1/N) sum_{n=1}^{N} mu(n) f(T^n x) converges to 0 as N -> infinity, where mu is the Mobius function. Equivalently, every zero-entropy dynamical system is disjoint from the Mobius function.
statement_informal: The Mobius function, which encodes information about prime factorizations, should be "unrelated" to any dynamical system with low complexity (zero topological entropy). Their correlation should vanish as you average over more and more terms.

statement:
  canonical: 'For every continuous function f on a compact metric space X and every
    homeomorphism T: X -> X with zero topological entropy, and for every point x in
    X, the sum (1/N) sum_{n=1}^{N} mu(n) f(T^n x) converges to 0 as N -> infinity,
    where mu is the Mobius function. Equivalently, every zero-entropy dynamical system
    is disjoint from the Mobius function.

    '
  informal: 'The Mobius function, which encodes information about prime factorizations,
    should be "unrelated" to any dynamical system with low complexity (zero topological
    entropy). Their correlation should vanish as you average over more and more terms.

    '

title: Generalized Sato-Tate Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let A be an abelian variety of dimension g over a number field. The normalized Frobenius angles (or normalized L-function coefficients) at primes of good reduction are equidistributed with respect to the Haar measure on a specific compact group ST(A), the Sato-Tate group of A. For elliptic curves without CM, this was proved to be SU(2) (the classical Sato-Tate). For higher-dimensional abelian varieties, the identity of ST(A) and the equidistribution remain conjectural in general.
statement_informal: The classical Sato-Tate conjecture (now a theorem for elliptic curves over totally real fields) predicts the distribution of point counts on elliptic curves mod p. The generalization to higher-dimensional abelian varieties and more general motives remains open.

title: Schanuel's Conjecture
statement_canonical: If z_1, z_2, ..., z_n are complex numbers that are linearly independent over the rational numbers Q, then the field extension Q(z_1, ..., z_n, e^{z_1}, ..., e^{z_n}) has transcendence degree at least n over Q.
statement_informal: Given n complex numbers that are linearly independent over the rationals, the 2n numbers formed by these numbers together with their exponentials contain at least n that are algebraically independent. This would settle nearly all open problems in transcendental number theory.

title: Schinzel's Hypothesis H
statement_canonical: Let f_1(x), ..., f_s(x) be irreducible polynomials with integer coefficients and positive leading coefficients. Suppose that no prime p divides the product f_1(n)*...*f_s(n) for all integers n (the "no fixed prime divisor" condition). Then there exist infinitely many positive integers n such that f_1(n), ..., f_s(n) are all simultaneously prime.
statement_informal: Any collection of integer polynomials that could conceivably all be prime at the same time (no obvious obstruction from a single prime dividing the product) will indeed all be simultaneously prime for infinitely many inputs.

  (n^2+1 primes), Bunyakovsky''s conjecture, and many others. It remains completely

  linear are unproven (e.g., n^2+1).

title: Scholz Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let l(n) denote the length of the shortest addition chain for the positive integer n (the minimum number of additions needed to reach n starting from 1, where each step adds two previously computed values). Then l(2^n - 1) <= l(n) + n - 1 for all positive integers n.
statement_informal: An addition chain for n is a sequence starting at 1 where each term is the sum of two earlier terms, ending at n. The conjecture says that computing 2^n - 1 (a Mersenne number) never requires more than n - 1 extra steps beyond what is needed to compute n itself.

title: Selberg Eigenvalue Conjecture
statement_canonical: For any congruence subgroup Gamma of SL(2, Z), every nonzero eigenvalue lambda of the Laplacian on the modular surface Gamma\H satisfies lambda >= 1/4, where H is the upper half-plane. Equivalently, for Maass cusp forms on congruence subgroups, the spectral parameter satisfies |Re(s) - 1/2| = 0 (i.e., s lies on the critical line Re(s) = 1/2).
statement_informal: The Laplacian on modular surfaces coming from congruence subgroups should have no "small eigenvalues" — all non-trivial eigenvalues should be at least 1/4. This is an analogue of the Ramanujan conjecture for non-holomorphic modular forms.

title: Selfridge's Conjecture on Primality
statement_canonical: There is no composite number n that is simultaneously a base-2 Fermat pseudoprime (2^{n-1} ≡ 1 mod n) and a Lucas pseudoprime with respect to some Lucas sequence with Jacobi symbol parameters chosen appropriately. Equivalently, there is no PSW pseudoprime: no composite passes both a strong base-2 probable prime test and a strong Lucas probable prime test with the standard parameter selection.
statement_informal: The Pomerance-Selfridge-Wagstaff (PSW) conjecture says that no composite number can fool both a Fermat primality test with base 2 and a Lucas primality test at the same time. If true, the combination would be a deterministic primality test.

statement:
  canonical: 'There is no composite number n that is simultaneously a base-2 Fermat
    pseudoprime (2^{n-1} ≡ 1 mod n) and a Lucas pseudoprime with respect to some Lucas
    sequence with Jacobi symbol parameters chosen appropriately. Equivalently, there
    is no PSW pseudoprime: no composite passes both a strong base-2 probable prime
    test and a strong Lucas probable prime test with the standard parameter selection.

    '
  informal: 'The Pomerance-Selfridge-Wagstaff (PSW) conjecture says that no composite
    number can fool both a Fermat primality test with base 2 and a Lucas primality
    test at the same time. If true, the combination would be a deterministic primality
    test.

    '

title: Sendov's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let p(z) be a polynomial of degree n >= 2 with all zeros contained in the closed unit disk |z| <= 1. Then for each zero a of p, there exists a critical point w (zero of p') with |a - w| <= 1. That is, every zero of p has a critical point of p within distance 1 from it.
statement_informal: Consider a polynomial whose roots all lie within the unit circle in the complex plane. Sendov conjectured that near each root, there is always a critical point (a place where the derivative vanishes) within distance 1. Despite its elementary statement, this conjecture has been open since 1958.

title: Seymour's Second Neighborhood Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every finite simple directed graph (with no loops or parallel edges) has a vertex v whose second neighborhood (the set of vertices reachable from v by a directed path of length exactly 2, but not directly from v) is at least as large as its first neighborhood (out-neighborhood). That is, there exists v with |N^+(N^+(v)) \ N^+(v)| >= |N^+(v)|.
statement_informal: In any directed graph, there is always some vertex that has at least as many "friends of friends" (reachable in exactly two steps, excluding direct neighbors) as it has direct friends (outgoing neighbors).

title: Shelah's Categoricity Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let K be an abstract elementary class (AEC) with amalgamation. If K is categorical in some cardinal lambda >= Hanf(K) (meaning K has exactly one model of size lambda up to isomorphism), then K is categorical in all cardinals mu >= Hanf(K). This would generalize Morley's categoricity theorem from first-order logic to the much broader framework of abstract elementary classes.
statement_informal: In first-order logic, Morley proved that if a theory has exactly one model of some uncountable size, it has exactly one model of every uncountable size. Shelah conjectured this "transfer of uniqueness" should hold in a much more general logical setting called abstract elementary classes, which encompass many non-first-order logics.

title: Sierpinski Problem
statement_canonical: The Sierpinski problem asks to determine the smallest odd positive integer k such that k * 2^n + 1 is composite for all positive integers n. It is conjectured that the smallest such Sierpinski number is k = 78557. This requires showing that for every odd k < 78557 that is not already eliminated by covering set arguments, there exists some n for which k * 2^n + 1 is prime.
statement_informal: A Sierpinski number is an odd number k for which k * 2^n + 1 is never prime, no matter what n you choose. It is known that 78557 is a Sierpinski number (it has a covering set). The problem is to prove it is the smallest one, by finding a prime of the form k * 2^n + 1 for each remaining candidate k.

title: Simplex Conjecture in Information Theory
statement_canonical: Among all codes (finite subsets) of M points on the unit sphere S^{n-1} in R^n, the configuration that maximizes the minimum pairwise distance is the regular simplex when M = n+1. For general M > n+1, the conjecture extends to the arrangement maximizing the minimum distance, which remains open in most dimensions for M not equal to special values. In the information-theoretic formulation, the simplex code achieves the optimal error exponent for equal-energy codes on the Gaussian channel.
statement_informal: When packing M points on a sphere to maximize the minimum distance between any two points, the optimal arrangement for n+1 points in n dimensions forms a regular simplex. The problem is open for many other values of M and n.

title: Singmaster's Conjecture
statement_canonical: There exists a finite upper bound N such that every integer greater than 1 appears at most N times in Pascal's triangle. Equivalently, for each integer k > 1, the number of solutions to the equation C(n, m) = k (with 0 < m < n) is bounded by a universal constant. Singmaster conjectured N = 8, the multiplicity achieved by the number 3003.
statement_informal: In Pascal's triangle, each number appears some number of times. The number 1 appears infinitely often (it borders both sides), but every other number seems to appear only finitely many times. The conjecture says there is a universal upper bound on how many times any number greater than 1 can appear.

title: Slice-Ribbon Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every slice knot is a ribbon knot. A knot K in S^3 is slice if it bounds a smoothly embedded disk D^2 in the 4-ball B^4. It is ribbon if it bounds an immersed disk in S^3 whose only singularities are "ribbon singularities" (the disk passes through itself in arcs, with no "clasp" singularities). The conjecture asserts that these two conditions are equivalent.
statement_informal: A knot is "slice" if it can bound a smooth disk in 4-dimensional space. A knot is "ribbon" if it can bound a disk that crosses itself only in a simple way (ribbon crossings). The conjecture says these two notions are the same: any knot that bounds a disk in 4D can do so without complicated self-intersections.

title: Smale's 17th Problem
statement_canonical: Can a zero of a system of n polynomial equations in n complex unknowns be found approximately in time polynomial in the size of the input (the number of coefficients)? More precisely, is there a uniform algorithm that, given a system f = (f_1, ..., f_n) of polynomials, produces an approximate zero in expected time polynomial in N = sum of the number of monomials, with the expectation over a suitable probability distribution?
statement_informal: Is there an efficient algorithm to find approximate solutions to systems of polynomial equations? Smale asks whether this can be done in polynomial time on average.

title: Smooth 4-Dimensional Poincare Conjecture

title: Strong Cosmic Censorship Conjecture (Mathematical Formulation)
statement_canonical: The maximal globally hyperbolic development of generic compact or asymptotically flat initial data for the Einstein vacuum equations R_{μν} = 0 is inextendible as a suitably regular Lorentzian manifold. In Christodoulou's C^0 formulation: the maximal Cauchy development is C^0-inextendible for generic initial data.
statement_informal: The laws of general relativity, given generic initial conditions, should determine the spacetime uniquely — there should be no way to extend the solution beyond a certain boundary (the Cauchy horizon) in a physically meaningful way. This is the mathematical version of the statement that "physics is deterministic in general relativity."

    as a suitably regular Lorentzian manifold. In Christodoulou''s C^0 formulation:
    the maximal Cauchy development is C^0-inextendible for generic initial data.

    determine the spacetime uniquely — there should be no way to extend the solution
    beyond a certain boundary (the Cauchy horizon) in a physically meaningful way.
    This is the mathematical version of the statement that "physics is deterministic
    in general relativity."

    '

title: Tate Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let X be a smooth projective variety over a finitely generated field k, and let l be a prime different from the characteristic of k. The cycle class map from algebraic cycles modulo homological equivalence tensored with Q_l to the l-adic cohomology group H^{2i}(X_{\bar{k}}, Q_l(i))^{Gal(\bar{k}/k)} is surjective. Equivalently, every Galois-invariant cohomology class in the appropriate degree comes from an algebraic cycle.
statement_informal: For varieties over finite fields or number fields, every cohomology class that is fixed by the Galois symmetry group actually comes from a geometric object (an algebraic cycle).

  geometry, parallel to the Hodge conjecture in complex geometry. It has profound
  implications for the theory of motives, for the Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture,
  and for the Langlands program. It remains open in general, though proved in many
  important special cases.

  '

title: Telescope Conjecture
statement_canonical: In stable homotopy theory, the telescope conjecture (Ravenel, 1984) asserts that the v_n-periodic homotopy groups of a finite complex X, computed via the telescope (direct limit of v_n self-maps), agree with the L_n-local homotopy groups (computed via Bousfield localization at the Johnson-Wilson theory E(n)). Equivalently, the natural map from the telescope to the L_n-localization is an equivalence.
statement_informal: There are two ways to isolate periodic phenomena in stable homotopy theory: one concrete (telescopes) and one abstract (Bousfield localization). The telescope conjecture predicted they agree. This was recently disproved at height 2 and above.

why_it_matters: 'The telescope conjecture was one of Ravenel''s conjectures that organized
  chromatic homotopy theory for 40 years. While five of Ravenel''s seven conjectures
  were proved by Devinatz, Hopkins, and Smith, the telescope conjecture was recently
  disproved by Burklund, Hahn, Levy, and Schlank (2023) at heights >= 2. Understanding
  the precise failure and its consequences for chromatic homotopy theory is a major
  open direction.

  '

title: Tight Closure Localization Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let R be a Noetherian ring of prime characteristic p > 0, I an ideal of R, and S a multiplicative set. If an element z belongs to the tight closure I^* of I in R, then the image of z in S^{-1}R belongs to the tight closure (S^{-1}I)^* of S^{-1}I in the localization S^{-1}R. That is, tight closure commutes with localization: (I^*)S^{-1}R = (IS^{-1}R)^*.
statement_informal: Tight closure is an important operation on ideals in commutative algebra defined using the Frobenius endomorphism in characteristic p. The conjecture says that this operation behaves well under localization — restricting to a smaller piece of the ring preserves the tight closure.

title: Total Coloring Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every simple graph G with maximum degree Delta(G), the total chromatic number chi''(G) satisfies chi''(G) <= Delta(G) + 2. The total chromatic number is the minimum number of colors needed to color both the vertices and edges of G so that no two adjacent or incident elements share the same color.
statement_informal: If you want to color both the vertices and edges of a graph so that no two neighboring elements share a color, you never need more than Delta + 2 colors, where Delta is the maximum degree.

title: Turan's Brick Factory Problem
statement_canonical: Determine the crossing number cr(K_{m,n}) of the complete bipartite graph K_{m,n}. The conjecture states that cr(K_{m,n}) = floor(m/2) * floor((m-1)/2) * floor(n/2) * floor((n-1)/2), which is the number of crossings in the natural drawing of K_{m,n}.
statement_informal: What is the minimum number of edge crossings when drawing the complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} in the plane? Turan conjectured that the obvious symmetric drawing is optimal.

title: Twin Prime Conjecture
statement_canonical: There are infinitely many primes p such that p + 2 is also prime. Equivalently, lim inf_{n -> infinity} (p_{n+1} - p_n) = 2, where p_n denotes the n-th prime number.
statement_informal: Twin primes are pairs of prime numbers that differ by exactly 2, such as (3, 5), (11, 13), and (17, 19). The twin prime conjecture asserts that there are infinitely many such pairs.

title: Uhl's Conjecture on Vector Measures
statement_canonical: A Banach space X has the Radon-Nikodym property (RNP) if and only if every X-valued measure of bounded variation that is absolutely continuous with respect to a scalar measure has a Radon-Nikodym derivative (density). Uhl's conjecture characterizes which Banach spaces have the RNP in terms of geometric properties: X has RNP if and only if every closed bounded convex subset of X is the closed convex hull of its strongly exposed points.
statement_informal: Which Banach spaces have the property that vector-valued measures always have densities? The conjecture connects this analytic property to the geometry of convex sets in the space.

title: Ungar's Conjecture on Direction Sets
statement_canonical: Let S be a set of n points in R^d (d >= 2), not all on a single hyperplane. The set of directions determined by pairs of points in S has cardinality at least 2*floor(n/2). In the plane (d=2), Ungar proved this bound of 2*floor(n/2) directions in 1982. The conjecture extends to higher dimensions: for d >= 3, what is the minimum number of directions determined by n points in general position in R^d?
statement_informal: Given n points in d-dimensional space not all on a hyperplane, how few distinct directions can the connecting lines determine? Ungar proved the 2D case; the higher-dimensional analogue is open.

title: Union-Closed Sets Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let F be a finite family of finite sets, not all empty, that is union-closed (i.e., the union of any two sets in F is also in F). Then there exists an element that belongs to at least half of the sets in F. Formally, there exists x in the union of F such that |{A in F : x in A}| >= |F|/2.
statement_informal: Consider a collection of sets that is closed under taking unions (combining any two sets in the collection produces another set that is also in the collection). The conjecture says there must be some element that appears in at least half the sets.

title: Unitary Dual Problem for Reductive Groups
statement_canonical: Classify all irreducible unitary representations of a real reductive Lie group G. That is, determine the unitary dual G-hat = {equivalence classes of irreducible unitary representations of G on Hilbert spaces} for all real reductive groups G. The problem remains open for general real reductive groups beyond the classical groups of small rank.
statement_informal: A "representation" describes how a symmetry group can act on a vector space by rotations and reflections. The unitary dual problem asks for a complete list of all the basic (irreducible) ways a given Lie group can act while preserving lengths and angles (unitarily). This is the fundamental classification problem in representation theory.

title: Vaught's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let T be a complete countable first-order theory in a countable language. Then the number of countable models of T up to isomorphism (denoted I(T, aleph_0)) is either countable (i.e., finite or equal to aleph_0) or exactly 2^{aleph_0} (the cardinality of the continuum). In other words, no complete countable first-order theory has exactly aleph_1 many countable models (assuming the continuum hypothesis fails).
statement_informal: When studying a mathematical theory (a consistent set of axioms in first-order logic), one can ask: how many essentially different countable structures satisfy these axioms? Vaught conjectured that the answer is either small (countably many) or as large as possible (continuum many) -- there is no intermediate case.

title: Vizing's Conjecture
statement_canonical: For any two graphs G and H, the domination number of the Cartesian product satisfies gamma(G square H) >= gamma(G) * gamma(H), where gamma denotes the domination number and G square H is the Cartesian product of graphs G and H.
statement_informal: The minimum number of vertices needed to dominate the Cartesian product of two graphs is at least the product of the domination numbers of the individual graphs. In other words, domination does not become easier in the product.

title: Volume Conjecture
statement_canonical: For any hyperbolic knot K in S^3, let J_N(K; q) denote the colored Jones polynomial colored by the N-dimensional irreducible representation of sl(2,C), evaluated at q = exp(2πi/N). Then lim_{N→∞} (2π/N) log |J_N(K; e^{2πi/N})| = vol(S^3 \ K), where vol denotes the simplicial (hyperbolic) volume of the knot complement.
statement_informal: The colored Jones polynomial is an algebraic knot invariant. The volume conjecture says that if you evaluate it at special roots of unity and take a limit, you recover the hyperbolic volume of the knot complement — linking quantum algebra to hyperbolic geometry.

title: Waring's Problem (Exact Values of g(k))
statement_canonical: Let g(k) denote the smallest s such that every positive integer can be written as a sum of at most s k-th powers of non-negative integers. It is conjectured that for all k >= 2, g(k) = 2^k + floor((3/2)^k) - 2, provided that the fractional part {(3/2)^k} satisfies {(3/2)^k} <= 1 - (3/4)^k + floor((3/4)^k) * 2^{-k}. Prove this formula for all k, or determine g(k) exactly for all k >= 2.
statement_informal: Waring's problem asks: for each power k, what is the maximum number of k-th powers needed to represent any positive integer as their sum? A conjectured formula gives the answer for all k, but proving it requires showing a certain inequality about powers of 3/2 holds for all k.

title: Wegner's Conjecture on Disk Graphs
statement_canonical: For every unit disk graph G with clique number ω(G), the chromatic number χ(G) satisfies χ(G) ≤ (3/2)ω(G) - 1/2 for ω(G) ≥ 3. More generally, for disk graphs (not necessarily unit), the chromatic number should be bounded by a function that is at most 3/2 times the clique number (up to lower-order terms).
statement_informal: If you represent a graph by disks in the plane where vertices are connected when their disks overlap, how many colors do you need? Wegner conjectured that the answer is at most about 3/2 times the size of the largest clique.

title: Weinstein Conjecture
statement_canonical: Every closed contact manifold (M, xi) admits at least one closed orbit of the Reeb vector field associated to any contact form alpha defining xi. Equivalently, for any contact form on a closed manifold, the Reeb flow has at least one periodic orbit.
statement_informal: On any compact odd-dimensional manifold equipped with a contact structure (a geometric structure arising naturally in mechanics and optics), there is always a trajectory of the associated dynamical system that returns to its starting point -- a periodic orbit must exist.
full_yaml:
