    of the Reeb vector field associated to any contact form alpha defining xi. Equivalently,
    for any contact form on a closed manifold, the Reeb flow has at least one periodic
    orbit.

    '

    (a geometric structure arising naturally in mechanics and optics), there is always
    a trajectory of the associated dynamical system that returns to its starting point
    -- a periodic orbit must exist.

    '

title: Weiss Conjecture on Admissibility
statement_canonical: Let A be the generator of a bounded analytic semigroup on a Hilbert space H, and let C: D(A) -> Y be an observation operator. Then C is an infinite-time admissible observation operator for the semigroup if and only if the Carleson measure condition holds: sup_{Re(s)>0} ||C(sI - A)^{-1}|| < infinity. In other words, resolvent boundedness characterizes admissibility.
statement_informal: For certain infinite-dimensional linear systems (e.g., heat equations, wave equations), the question is whether observability (can you recover the state from measurements?) is equivalent to a simple resolvent estimate.

title: Welsh's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let m_n denote the number of matroids on an n-element ground set. Welsh conjectured that the sequence log(m_n)/n^2 converges, and more generally asked for the asymptotic growth rate of the number of matroids. It is known that log(m_n)/n^2 is bounded between positive constants. The conjecture is that the limit exists and equals a specific constant.
statement_informal: How many matroids are there on n elements? The number grows super-exponentially, roughly as 2^{cn^2}. Welsh conjectured that the growth rate (in a logarithmic sense) converges to a precise constant.

title: Woodin's Ultimate L Conjecture
statement_canonical: There exists a canonical inner model (called "Ultimate L") that is compatible with all large cardinal axioms and satisfies V = Ultimate L. More precisely, assuming the existence of an extendible cardinal, there is a definable inner model L_Omega that is close to V (satisfies a strong form of the Weak Covering Lemma), is compatible with all large cardinals, and in which the Continuum Hypothesis and other combinatorial principles hold. The conjecture asserts that such a model exists and that V = L_Omega is consistent with all known large cardinal axioms.
statement_informal: The Ultimate L Conjecture proposes that there is a "best" canonical model of set theory that looks like Godel's constructible universe L but is large enough to accommodate all known large cardinal axioms. This would resolve the Continuum Hypothesis (it would be true in this model) and provide a definitive answer to "what sets exist."

  the Continuum Hypothesis. If successful, it would provide a canonical model of set
  theory compatible with all large cardinals, resolving questions left open since
  Cohen''s independence proof of 1963. It represents a competing approach to the forcing
  axiom/Martin''s Maximum program.

  '

title: Yau's Conjecture on the First Eigenvalue
statement_canonical: Let Sigma be a closed embedded minimal hypersurface in the unit sphere S^{n+1}. Then the first eigenvalue of the Laplacian on Sigma equals n, i.e., lambda_1(Sigma) = n. This is known to hold for totally geodesic equators (where lambda_1 = n) and the conjecture asserts it holds for all such embedded minimal hypersurfaces.
statement_informal: Minimal surfaces in spheres are surfaces that locally minimize area. Yau conjectured that for any such surface embedded (without self-intersections) in the sphere, the lowest nontrivial frequency of vibration (first eigenvalue of the Laplacian) takes a specific universal value that depends only on the dimension.

title: Zarankiewicz Problem
statement_canonical: Determine the exact value of the Zarankiewicz number z(m,n;s,t), defined as the maximum number of 1s in an m x n binary matrix that contains no s x t all-ones submatrix. Equivalently, determine the Turan number ex(n; K_{s,t}), the maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph on n+n vertices containing no K_{s,t} subgraph. In particular, the exact asymptotics of ex(n; K_{s,t}) for general s,t remain unknown.
statement_informal: How many edges can a bipartite graph have while avoiding a complete bipartite subgraph K_{s,t}? The Kovari-Sos-Turan theorem gives an upper bound, but matching constructions exist only for special cases.

  persisted for decades and resolving it would have major implications for Turan-type
  extremal problems.

  '

title: Zimmer's Conjecture
statement_canonical: Let Gamma be a lattice in a simple Lie group G of real rank r >= 2. If Gamma acts faithfully on a compact manifold M by C^2 diffeomorphisms, then dim(M) >= dim(G/P) where P is a maximal parabolic subgroup of G. In particular, for SL(n, Z) acting on a compact manifold M, one needs dim(M) >= n - 1.
statement_informal: Higher-rank lattices (such as SL(n,Z) for n >= 3) are very rigid algebraic objects. Zimmer's conjecture predicts that they cannot act nontrivially on manifolds that are "too small" in dimension -- there is a minimum dimension for the manifold that depends on the rank of the lattice.

title: 3SUM Complexity: Is There an O(n log n) Algorithm?
statement_canonical: The 3SUM problem asks: given a set S of n integers, do there exist three elements a, b, c in S such that a + b + c = 0? The classical algorithm solves this in O(n^2) time. The 3SUM conjecture asserts that no algorithm can solve 3SUM in O(n^{2-epsilon}) time for any constant epsilon > 0 (in the real RAM model or comparison-based model). Is there an algorithm for 3SUM running in time O(n log n), or more generally, in truly subquadratic time o(n^2)?
statement_informal: Given a list of n numbers, can we find three of them that add up to zero faster than checking almost all triples? The obvious approach takes time proportional to n-squared. Can we do significantly better, perhaps in time proportional to n times log(n)? Most researchers believe we cannot do much better than n-squared.

  would imply a truly subquadratic algorithm for 3SUM. The 3SUM conjecture is one
  of the key hypotheses (alongside SETH and APSP conjecture) used to establish conditional
  lower bounds throughout algorithm design. Understanding the true complexity of 3SUM
  would clarify the landscape of polynomial-time problems.

  '

title: Aanderaa-Karp-Rosenberg Conjecture (Evasiveness Conjecture)
statement_canonical: A graph property is a class of graphs closed under isomorphism. A graph property P on n vertices is called evasive if every deterministic algorithm that determines whether an input graph on n vertices has property P by querying individual edges must, in the worst case, query all n(n-1)/2 possible edges. The Aanderaa-Karp-Rosenberg conjecture (evasiveness conjecture) states that every nontrivial monotone graph property is evasive. A monotone graph property is one that is preserved under the addition of edges, and nontrivial means it is not satisfied by all graphs or by no graphs.
statement_informal: Suppose you want to determine whether a graph has some property (such as being connected, containing a triangle, or being planar) that is preserved when you add edges. You learn about the graph by asking about one edge at a time: "Is there an edge between vertex i and vertex j?" The conjecture says that for any such property, an adversary can always force you to ask about every possible edge before you can determine the answer.

title: Art Gallery Problem Computational Complexity
statement_canonical: The art gallery problem asks for the minimum number of guards needed to see every point of a simple polygon. While Chvátal's theorem gives an upper bound of floor(n/3) guards for an n-vertex polygon, the computational problem of finding the minimum number of guards is known to be NP-hard. What is the exact complexity? Is the problem in NP? Is it in the existential theory of the reals (ETR-complete)?

title: Berman-Hartmanis Isomorphism Conjecture
statement_canonical: The Berman-Hartmanis conjecture states that all NP-complete languages are polynomial-time isomorphic to each other. That is, for any two NP-complete languages A and B, there exists a polynomial-time computable bijection f with a polynomial-time computable inverse such that x is in A iff f(x) is in B.

title: BPP vs P Problem
statement_canonical: Let BPP (Bounded-error Probabilistic Polynomial time) denote the class of languages decidable by a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time with error probability at most 1/3 on every input. Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. It is known that P is contained in BPP. Does P = BPP? That is, can every efficient randomized algorithm be derandomized into an efficient deterministic algorithm?
statement_informal: Does access to random coin flips give computers any extra power for solving problems efficiently? Or can every problem that can be solved quickly with randomness also be solved quickly without it? Most experts believe randomness does not help — that P = BPP — but proving this remains open.

title: BQP vs NP Problem
statement_canonical: Let BQP (Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time) denote the class of decision problems solvable by a quantum computer in polynomial time with error probability at most 1/3. Let NP denote the class of decision problems for which a "yes" answer can be verified in polynomial time given a suitable certificate. What is the relationship between BQP and NP? Specifically, is NP contained in BQP, is BQP contained in NP, or are they incomparable?
statement_informal: Quantum computers can solve some problems faster than classical computers (like factoring integers). But can quantum computers efficiently solve all problems whose solutions can be quickly verified? In other words, can quantum computers crack NP-complete problems like the traveling salesman problem? Most experts believe quantum computers cannot solve all NP problems efficiently, but this has not been proven.

title: Optimal Cache-Oblivious Algorithms

title: Capacity-Achieving Code with Linear-Time Encoding and Decoding
statement_canonical: Shannon's channel coding theorem guarantees the existence of codes that achieve the capacity of any discrete memoryless channel with vanishing error probability as the block length tends to infinity. However, Shannon's proof is non-constructive. Does there exist an explicit family of codes that simultaneously: (1) achieves the capacity of the binary symmetric channel (or more generally, any discrete memoryless channel), (2) has a linear-time encoding algorithm (O(n) time for block length n), and (3) has a linear-time decoding algorithm (O(n) time for block length n)?
statement_informal: Shannon proved in 1948 that reliable communication over a noisy channel is possible up to a fundamental limit called the channel capacity. But can we build practical codes that reach this limit while being extremely fast — encoding and decoding each message in time proportional to its length?

  summary: 'This problem asks whether there exist explicit error-correcting codes
    that achieve Shannon channel capacity with both encoding and decoding running
    in linear time O(n).

    '
partial_results:
- summary: Gallager's LDPC codes (1962) empirically approach capacity and have near-linear
    decoding complexity under iterative message-passing decoding, but provable capacity-achievement
    with linear-time decoding is not established for general channels.

- summary: Arikan's polar codes (2009) provably achieve the capacity of any binary-input
    symmetric memoryless channel with encoding and decoding complexity O(n log n).
  year: 2009
- summary: Spielman showed how to construct linear-time encodable and decodable codes
    that correct a constant fraction of errors (for adversarial channels), but these
    do not achieve Shannon capacity.
  year: 1996
- summary: Pfister and Urbanke showed that spatially coupled LDPC codes universally
    achieve capacity on the BEC with near-linear complexity under belief propagation
    decoding.
  year: 2014
- summary: Polar codes with successive cancellation list decoding achieve competitive
    performance in 5G NR standards, with O(n log n) complexity.
  year: 2018

  near-linear complexity, achieving exact capacity with strictly linear-time encoding
  and decoding for general channels remains open. Such codes would have enormous practical
  impact in communications, storage, and any system where data must be transmitted
  reliably over noisy channels at the highest possible rate with minimal computational
  overhead.

  '

title: Circuit Complexity of Explicit Functions
statement_canonical: Prove a superlinear lower bound on the circuit complexity of an explicit Boolean function. Specifically, exhibit a function f in NP (or even in P) and prove that any Boolean circuit computing f requires more than C*n gates for every constant C, where n is the input length.
statement_informal: Can we prove that some specific, natural Boolean function requires large circuits to compute? Despite knowing that most functions require exponential-size circuits (by counting arguments), we cannot prove more than a roughly 3n lower bound for any explicit function.

title: Log-Rank Conjecture in Communication Complexity
statement_canonical: The log-rank conjecture (Lovász and Saks, 1988) states that the deterministic communication complexity of a Boolean function f(x,y) is at most polylogarithmic in the rank of its communication matrix M_f (where M_f[x,y] = f(x,y) over the reals). Is this conjecture true?
statement_informal: When two parties need to compute a joint function of their inputs by exchanging messages, is the amount of communication needed always bounded by a polynomial in the logarithm of the rank of the function's truth table viewed as a matrix?

title: Computational-Statistical Gap
statement_canonical: For many statistical estimation problems, the information-theoretically optimal sample complexity is much smaller than what any known polynomial-time algorithm achieves. Is there a fundamental computational-statistical gap, where polynomial-time algorithms provably require more samples than information-theoretic bounds? Can we prove such gaps unconditionally, or at least under standard complexity assumptions?

title: Dynamic Connectivity Optimal Bounds
statement_canonical: In the dynamic connectivity problem, we maintain an undirected graph under edge insertions and deletions while answering connectivity queries. Holm, de Lichtenberg, and Thorup (2001) achieved O(log^2 n) amortized time per operation. Kapron, King, and Mountjoy (2013) achieved O(log^4 n) worst-case. What are the optimal bounds for both amortized and worst-case dynamic connectivity?

title: Dynamic Optimality Conjecture for Binary Search Trees
statement_canonical: The dynamic optimality conjecture (Sleator and Tarjan, 1985) states that splay trees are O(1)-competitive with any offline binary search tree algorithm. That is, for any sequence of accesses, the total cost of splay tree operations is at most a constant factor times the cost of the optimal offline BST for that sequence. Is this conjecture true?
statement_informal: Is the splay tree always within a constant factor of the best possible binary search tree for any access sequence, even compared to one that knows the future?

title: ETH vs SETH Gap
statement_canonical: The Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) states that 3-SAT cannot be solved in 2^o(n) time. The Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH) states that for every epsilon > 0, there exists k such that k-SAT cannot be solved in O(2^((1-epsilon)n)) time. Does ETH imply SETH, or are they independent? More broadly, what is the relationship between these hypotheses and their fine-grained consequences?
statement_informal: Are the two main hardness assumptions in fine-grained complexity theory (ETH and SETH) equivalent, or does the stronger one (SETH) really add power? What are the exact consequences of each assumption?

title: Existence of One-Way Functions
statement_canonical: A function f: {0,1}* -> {0,1}* is called a one-way function if (1) f is computable in polynomial time, and (2) for every probabilistic polynomial-time algorithm A, the probability that A(f(x), 1^|x|) outputs some x' with f(x') = f(x) is negligible in |x|, where x is chosen uniformly at random. Do one-way functions exist? Equivalently, does there exist a polynomial-time computable function that cannot be inverted by any probabilistic polynomial-time algorithm with non-negligible probability?
statement_informal: A one-way function is easy to compute but hard to reverse. For example, multiplying two large prime numbers is easy, but finding the prime factors of the product is believed to be hard. Do functions with this property actually exist, or can every efficiently computable function be efficiently inverted? The entire field of cryptography rests on the assumption that such functions exist.

    that cannot be inverted by any probabilistic polynomial-time algorithm with non-negligible
    probability?

    '
  informal: 'A one-way function is easy to compute but hard to reverse. For example,
    multiplying two large prime numbers is easy, but finding the prime factors of
    the product is believed to be hard. Do functions with this property actually exist,
    or can every efficiently computable function be efficiently inverted? The entire
    field of cryptography rests on the assumption that such functions exist.

    '

title: Existence of One-Way Functions
verification_mode: proof
verification_profile:
  machine_actionability: low
  solution_checkability: proof_assistant
  statement_precision: high
why_it_matters: 'The existence of one-way functions is the minimal assumption on which
  almost all of modern cryptography is built. Impagliazzo and Luby showed that one-way
  functions exist if and only if pseudorandom generators exist, which in turn are
  equivalent to the existence of pseudorandom functions, commitment schemes, and digital
  signatures. The existence of one-way functions implies P != NP, but P != NP alone
  is not known to imply the existence of one-way functions. Resolving this question
  would either validate or invalidate the theoretical foundations of cryptography.

  '

statement_canonical: A Boolean formula is a circuit where every gate has fan-out one. It is known that formulas can be at most quadratically less efficient than circuits. Is there an explicit function with polynomial-size circuits but requiring super-polynomial-size formulas? Equivalently, is NC^1 strictly contained in P/poly?

title: Graph Isomorphism Problem Complexity
statement_canonical: The graph isomorphism problem (GI) asks, given two finite graphs G and H, whether there exists a bijection between their vertex sets that preserves adjacency. GI is known to be in NP but is neither known to be in P nor known to be NP-complete. What is the precise computational complexity of graph isomorphism? Specifically, is GI in P, or is it NP-complete, or does it have intermediate complexity?
statement_informal: Given two networks (graphs), is there a way to relabel the nodes of one so that it becomes identical to the other? We know this can be checked quickly once a relabeling is proposed, but we do not know whether the relabeling can always be found quickly. Is this problem fundamentally hard (NP-complete), efficiently solvable (in P), or something in between?

  neither known to be in P nor known to be NP-complete, making it a prime candidate
  for an NP-intermediate problem (which must exist if P != NP by Ladner''s theorem).
  Its complexity status has implications for group theory, combinatorics, database
  theory, and chemistry (molecule comparison). Babai''s 2015 quasipolynomial-time
  algorithm was a major breakthrough, but the gap between quasipolynomial and polynomial
  time remains.

  '

title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption Efficiency
statement_canonical: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) allows computation on encrypted data without decryption. Current FHE schemes incur significant overhead: ciphertext expansion is at least polylogarithmic and computation is orders of magnitude slower than plaintext computation. What is the minimum possible overhead for FHE? Can we achieve FHE with O(1) ciphertext expansion and computation overhead polynomial in the security parameter but independent of the computation depth?

title: Optimal k-Clique Detection Algorithm
statement_canonical: What is the optimal time complexity for detecting whether an n-vertex graph contains a clique of size k? For k=3 (triangle detection), the best known algorithm runs in O(n^omega) time where omega is the matrix multiplication exponent. For general k, can we do significantly better than n^(omega*k/3) time? Is there an algorithm running in n^(o(k)) time?
statement_informal: How fast can we find a clique of size k in a graph? The naive approach takes about n^k time. Can we do much better, especially for large k?

title: Kolmogorov Complexity and Cryptography
statement_canonical: Time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity Kt(x) measures the minimum description length of x among programs running in time t. Liu and Pass (2020) showed that the average-case hardness of computing Kt implies one-way functions. Is the converse true? Does the existence of one-way functions imply that Kt is hard on average? More broadly, what is the precise relationship between Kolmogorov complexity and cryptographic hardness?
statement_informal: Is the difficulty of compressing strings (computing Kolmogorov complexity) exactly equivalent to the existence of cryptographic hardness? Can we fully characterize cryptographic primitives through the lens of Kolmogorov complexity?

title: L vs NL Problem

statement_informal: Can every problem solvable with a small amount of nondeterministic memory also be solved with the same amount of deterministic memory? The canonical NL-complete problem is directed graph reachability (st-connectivity).

title: Matrix Multiplication Exponent
statement_canonical: Define the matrix multiplication exponent omega as the infimum over all real numbers c such that two n x n matrices over a field can be multiplied using O(n^c) arithmetic operations. It is known that 2 <= omega < 2.372. What is the exact value of omega? In particular, does omega = 2?
statement_informal: Multiplying two n-by-n matrices the standard way takes about n^3 operations. Strassen showed in 1969 that it can be done faster. The fundamental question is: what is the best possible exponent? Can we multiply matrices in essentially n^2 operations (which is optimal since just reading the input takes n^2 time), or is the true exponent strictly greater than 2?

title: Minimum Spanning Tree Optimal Comparison Complexity
statement_canonical: What is the optimal number of comparisons needed to find the minimum spanning tree of a weighted graph? Chazelle (2000) gave a nearly-linear time algorithm running in O(m * alpha(m,n)) time. Pettie and Ramachandran (2002) gave an optimal algorithm in the comparison model, but its complexity is unknown -- it runs in O(T*(m,n)) time where T* is the optimal decision tree complexity for MST, which is not known to be linear. Is the optimal MST comparison complexity O(m)?
statement_informal: What is the minimum number of comparisons needed to find a minimum spanning tree? We know the answer is close to linear, but whether it is exactly linear is unknown.

title: Natural Proofs Barrier Circumvention
statement_canonical: Razborov and Rudich (1997) showed that any proof of circuit lower bounds that is both "constructive" (can recognize hard functions efficiently) and "large" (applies to a large fraction of functions) cannot prove P != NP if one-way functions exist. Can we develop proof techniques that circumvent the natural proofs barrier to prove strong circuit lower bounds?
statement_informal: Most known techniques for proving circuit lower bounds have a special structure (they are "natural proofs") that provably cannot work to separate P from NP if cryptographic assumptions hold. Can we find fundamentally different proof strategies?

Or does the curse of dimensionality make this fundamentally impossible?

title: NP vs co-NP Problem
statement_canonical: Let NP denote the class of languages for which membership can be verified in polynomial time given a suitable certificate, and let co-NP denote the class of languages whose complements are in NP (equivalently, languages for which non-membership can be verified in polynomial time). Does NP = co-NP?
statement_informal: For problems where "yes" answers have short proofs that can be checked quickly (NP problems), do "no" answers also always have short proofs that can be checked quickly? For example, we can quickly verify that a number is composite by exhibiting a factor, but can we always quickly verify that a number is prime by providing a short proof of primality? (This particular example was resolved — primality is in P — but the general question remains open.)

    "yes" answers also has efficiently verifiable "no" answers, with deep implications
    for proof complexity and cryptography.

    '
partial_results:

    obviously imply NP != co-NP.
  year: 1971

    AKS showed primality is in P, but these results do not resolve the general NP
    vs co-NP question.

    proof systems (resolution, bounded-depth Frege), but not for general proof systems.
  year: 1985

    for the pigeonhole principle, contributing to proof complexity understanding.
  year: 2003

  propositional proof systems and the foundations of cryptography.

title: Online Bipartite Matching Optimal Competitive Ratio
statement_canonical: In the online bipartite matching problem, vertices on one side arrive online and must be irrevocably matched to previously arrived vertices. For the general weighted (edge-weighted) online bipartite matching problem, what is the optimal competitive ratio? Can 1 - 1/e be achieved in the weighted setting?

title: Online Learning with Expert Advice Optimal Bounds

Can we achieve computationally efficient algorithms matching the information-theoretic optimum?
statement_informal: When learning from experts over time, we want to perform nearly as well as the best expert in hindsight. For many natural variants of this problem, the exact optimal performance and efficient algorithms are still unknown.

title: P vs NP Problem
statement_canonical: Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, and NP denote the class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time (equivalently, languages for which a proposed solution can be verified in polynomial time). Does P = NP?
statement_informal: If a solution to a problem can be quickly verified (in polynomial time), can the solution itself always be quickly found (in polynomial time)? In other words, is efficient verification of answers just as powerful as efficient computation of answers?
full_yaml:
ai:
  recommended_tools:
  - python
  - lean
  solver_ready: false
aliases:
- P = NP?
- Cook's Problem
- P versus NP
answer_type: boolean
dates:
  first_stated: 1971
domain: theoretical-cs
formalization:
  available: false
  systems: []
  wanted: true
id: opa.tcs.complexity-theory.p-vs-np
kind: problem
machine_generated:
  decomposition:
  - Develop new circuit lower bound techniques beyond relativization, natural proofs,
    and algebrization barriers
  - Explore connections between proof complexity, algebraic geometry, and combinatorial
    optimization
  disclaimer: unverified
  summary: 'The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified
    in polynomial time can also be solved in polynomial time, and is the most important
    open question in computational complexity theory.

    '
partial_results:
- summary: Baker, Gill, and Solovay proved that there exist oracles A and B such that
    P^A = NP^A and P^B != NP^B, showing that any proof resolving P vs NP must be non-relativizing.
  year: 1975
- summary: Razborov and Rudich showed that 'natural proofs' cannot prove P != NP if
    one-way functions exist, ruling out a large class of proof strategies.
  year: 1997
- summary: Aaronson and Wigderson showed that the algebrization barrier further restricts
    the class of techniques that can resolve P vs NP.
  year: 2009
- summary: Ryan Williams proved that NEXP is not in ACC^0, the strongest circuit lower
    bound known, using a connection between algorithms and circuit lower bounds.
  year: 2011
prerequisites:
- computational complexity theory
- Turing machines
- nondeterminism
- NP-completeness
- polynomial-time reductions
prize:
  amount: '1000000'

statement:
  canonical: 'Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing
    machine in polynomial time, and NP denote the class of languages decidable by
    a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time (equivalently, languages
    for which a proposed solution can be verified in polynomial time). Does P = NP?

    '
  informal: 'If a solution to a problem can be quickly verified (in polynomial time),
    can the solution itself always be quickly found (in polynomial time)? In other
    words, is efficient verification of answers just as powerful as efficient computation
    of answers?

    '

why_it_matters: 'The P vs NP problem is widely regarded as the most important open
  problem in theoretical computer science and one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems.
  A proof that P = NP would revolutionize cryptography (rendering most current encryption
  insecure), optimization, artificial intelligence, and mathematics. A proof that
  P != NP would formally justify the belief that many important computational problems
  are inherently intractable and would place fundamental limits on what can be efficiently
  computed.

  '

title: P vs PSPACE Problem
statement_canonical: Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, and let PSPACE denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine using polynomial space. It is known that P is contained in PSPACE. Does P = PSPACE? Equivalently, can every problem solvable with polynomial space also be solved in polynomial time?
statement_informal: We know that problems solvable quickly (polynomial time) can also be solved with limited memory (polynomial space). But is the reverse true? Can every problem that requires only a reasonable amount of memory also be solved quickly? Most researchers believe the answer is no, but this remains unproven.

  time and space as computational resources. PSPACE includes problems from game theory
  (determining optimal strategies in two-player games like generalized chess), formal
  verification, and planning. A separation would prove that there are problems solvable
  with limited memory that inherently require exponential time, establishing a fundamental
  gap between these two resources. Since NP is contained in PSPACE, proving P != PSPACE
  would immediately imply P != NP.

  '

title: Is the Polynomial Hierarchy Infinite?
statement_canonical: The polynomial hierarchy (PH) is defined as the union of the classes Sigma_k^P for k >= 0, where Sigma_0^P = P, and Sigma_{k+1}^P = NP^{Sigma_k^P}. The hierarchy is said to collapse to level k if Sigma_k^P = Sigma_{k+1}^P (equivalently, if PH = Sigma_k^P). Is the polynomial hierarchy infinite, i.e., is Sigma_k^P strictly contained in Sigma_{k+1}^P for every k >= 0?
statement_informal: The polynomial hierarchy is a tower of complexity classes built on top of P and NP, where each level adds another layer of alternating "guessing." Does this tower go on forever with each level being strictly more powerful than the one below it, or does it eventually flatten out (collapse) so that additional levels of guessing add no more power?

    to Sigma_2^P.

    to Sigma_k^P.

statement:
  canonical: 'The polynomial hierarchy (PH) is defined as the union of the classes
    Sigma_k^P for k >= 0, where Sigma_0^P = P, and Sigma_{k+1}^P = NP^{Sigma_k^P}.
    The hierarchy is said to collapse to level k if Sigma_k^P = Sigma_{k+1}^P (equivalently,
    if PH = Sigma_k^P). Is the polynomial hierarchy infinite, i.e., is Sigma_k^P strictly
    contained in Sigma_{k+1}^P for every k >= 0?

    '
  informal: 'The polynomial hierarchy is a tower of complexity classes built on top
    of P and NP, where each level adds another layer of alternating "guessing." Does
    this tower go on forever with each level being strictly more powerful than the
    one below it, or does it eventually flatten out (collapse) so that additional
    levels of guessing add no more power?

    '

title: Is the Polynomial Hierarchy Infinite?
verification_mode: proof

  machine_actionability: low
  solution_checkability: proof_assistant
  statement_precision: high
why_it_matters: 'The polynomial hierarchy is one of the central structures in computational
  complexity theory. Many natural computational problems are complete for various
  levels of PH. If PH collapses, it would have dramatic consequences for the landscape
  of complexity theory — for instance, if PH collapses to P, then P = NP. Conversely,
  proving PH is infinite would separate P from NP (since P = NP implies PH = P). The
  question connects to circuit complexity, counting complexity, and the relativization
  barrier.

  '

title: Quantum Error Correction Threshold (Exact)
statement_canonical: The threshold theorem for quantum computation states that if the error rate per gate is below a threshold p_th, arbitrary long quantum computations can be performed with polynomial overhead. What is the exact value of p_th for the most general noise model? Current estimates range from 10^-4 to 10^-2 depending on the model and error correction code.
statement_informal: How noisy can quantum gates be while still allowing reliable quantum computation through error correction? We know a threshold exists, but its exact value depends on details that are not fully understood.

title: Quantum Interactive Proofs Structure

title: Quantum PCP Conjecture
statement_canonical: The classical PCP theorem states that every language in NP has a probabilistically checkable proof (PCP) where the verifier reads only a constant number of bits of the proof. The quantum PCP conjecture asks for an analogous result in the quantum setting: is it QMA-hard to approximate the ground state energy of a local Hamiltonian to within a constant fraction of the total number of terms? Formally, does there exist a constant epsilon > 0 such that the following promise problem is QMA-hard: given a k-local Hamiltonian H = sum_{i=1}^m H_i on n qubits (where each H_i acts on at most k qubits and 0 <= H_i <= I), distinguish between the cases where the ground state energy is at most epsilon * m and at least (1 - epsilon) * m?
statement_informal: The classical PCP theorem says you can verify any mathematical proof by reading only a few random bits of it. The quantum PCP conjecture asks whether a similar phenomenon holds for quantum proofs: is it computationally hard even to approximately determine the lowest energy state of a quantum system, even when "approximately" means just getting the answer roughly right?

    (the detectability lemma), a key ingredient in classical PCP proofs.

    LDPC codes, removing a major barrier to the quantum PCP conjecture.

title: Range Searching Lower Bounds
statement_canonical: In range searching, we preprocess n points in R^d to answer queries asking how many points lie inside a given geometric range (halfspace, simplex, box, etc.). What are tight lower bounds for the space-query time tradeoff? For simplex range searching in d >= 3 dimensions, is the known O(n/sqrt(m)) query time with O(m) space (for m >= n) tight? Can we prove matching lower bounds?
statement_informal: How efficiently can we count points inside geometric shapes after preprocessing? We have good algorithms, but proving they cannot be improved requires lower bounds that are largely missing.

title: Rectilinear Steiner Tree Problem
statement_canonical: The rectilinear Steiner tree problem asks for the shortest tree connecting a given set of points in the plane using only horizontal and vertical line segments, where additional Steiner points may be introduced. What is the best polynomial-time approximation ratio achievable? The current best is a PTAS by Arora (1998), but can the exact problem be solved faster in practice, and what are tight bounds for specific approximation schemes?
statement_informal: What is the shortest way to connect a set of points using only horizontal and vertical lines, when we are allowed to add extra junction points? This problem arises in VLSI chip design.

title: Secretary Problem Variants
statement_canonical: The matroid secretary conjecture (Babaioff, Immorlica, Kleinberg, 2007) states that for any matroid, there exists an O(1)-competitive online algorithm for the secretary problem defined over that matroid. That is, elements arrive in random order with adversarial weights, and the algorithm must select an independent set of maximum weight, achieving a constant fraction of the offline optimum. Is this true?
statement_informal: In the classical secretary problem, the best candidate can be found with probability 1/e. For generalizations involving complex combinatorial constraints (matroids), can we still achieve a constant-factor approximation?

title: Optimal Depth of Sorting Networks
statement_canonical: A sorting network is an oblivious comparison-based sorting algorithm with a fixed comparison pattern. title: Streaming Lower Bounds for Frequency Moments

title: Strongly Polynomial Algorithm for Linear Programming
statement_canonical: A linear program (LP) is the problem of optimizing a linear objective function subject to linear inequality and equality constraints: minimize c^T x subject to Ax <= b, x >= 0, where A is an m x n matrix. A strongly polynomial algorithm is one whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in n and m alone, independent of the bit-lengths of the coefficients in A, b, and c. Known polynomial-time algorithms for LP (ellipsoid method, interior-point methods) are weakly polynomial — their running time depends on the bit-length of the input. Does there exist a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for linear programming?
statement_informal: Linear programming is the problem of finding the best outcome in a mathematical model with linear constraints. We know how to solve it efficiently, but all known methods have running times that depend on the sizes of the numbers in the input, not just on the number of variables and constraints. Can we find a method whose speed depends only on the number of variables and constraints?

    algorithm whose runtime depends only on the number of variables and constraints,
    not on the magnitudes of the input numbers.

    '

    (1984) gave weakly polynomial algorithms for LP, resolving the question of polynomial-time
    solvability but not strong polynomiality.
  year: 1984

    a polynomial in n and m for LP with integer data, but each operation may involve
    large numbers.
  year: 1986

    minimum cost flow (via combinatorial algorithms).'

    log(n/delta)) pivots with high probability, but it is not strongly polynomial.
  year: 2020

title: Union-Find Optimal Bounds
statement_canonical: The Union-Find (disjoint set) data structure supports union and find operations. Tarjan (1975) showed that union by rank with path compression achieves O(alpha(n)) amortized time per operation, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function. Fredman and Saks (1989) proved an Omega(alpha(n)) lower bound in the cell probe model. Is this tight in more restrictive but natural models? What are the optimal bounds for concurrent and cache-oblivious variants?
statement_informal: The union-find data structure is essentially optimal for the pointer machine model, but what about for modern computing models that account for parallelism, caching, and concurrency?

title: Unique Games Conjecture
statement_canonical: For every constant epsilon > 0, the following promise problem is NP-hard: given a unique game instance (a constraint satisfaction problem where each constraint is a bijection between label sets of size k), distinguish between the case where at least (1 - epsilon) fraction of constraints can be satisfied and the case where at most epsilon fraction of constraints can be satisfied. Formally, for all epsilon > 0, there exists a label set size k = k(epsilon) such that it is NP-hard to (1 - epsilon, epsilon)-decide the Unique Label Cover problem with label set size k.
statement_informal: Consider a system of "unique" constraints where each constraint relates two variables via a one-to-one mapping over a set of labels. The conjecture says that even approximately deciding whether nearly all constraints can be satisfied (versus almost none can be) is computationally intractable (NP-hard).

    '

    algorithms.

    (a weaker variant of UGC).

    games.

why_it_matters: 'The Unique Games Conjecture (UGC), proposed by Subhash Khot in 2002,
  has become one of the central conjectures in computational complexity and approximation
  algorithms. If true, it would provide tight inapproximability results for a wide
  range of optimization problems, including Max-Cut, Vertex Cover, and constraint
  satisfaction problems. It would unify and strengthen many known hardness-of-approximation
  results. The conjecture led Khot to receive the Nevanlinna Prize in 2014 for its
  transformative impact on the field.

  '

title: VP vs VNP Problem
statement_canonical: Let VP denote the class of polynomial families computable by arithmetic circuits of polynomial size, and VNP denote Valiant's class of polynomial families that are definable as exponential sums over VP families (analogous to NP). Does VP = VNP?
statement_informal: Is the permanent polynomial as easy to compute as the determinant? This is the algebraic analogue of P vs NP, asking whether every polynomial with a short description also has a short computation.
